Our primary contribution is MEMSAD (Semantic Anomaly Detection), a calibration-based defense grounded in a gradient coupling theorem: under encoder regularity, the anomaly score gradient and the retrieval objective gradient are provably identical, so any continuous perturbation that reduces detection risk necessarily degrades retrieval rank. This coupling yields a certified detection radius guaranteeing correct classification regardless of adversary strategy. We prove minimax optimality via Le Cam's method, showing any threshold detector requires $\Omega(1/\rho^2)$ calibration samples and MEMSAD achieves this up to $\log(1/\delta)$ factors. We further derive online regret bounds for rolling calibration at rate $O(\sigma^{2/3}\Delta^{1/3})$, and formally characterize a discrete synonym-invariance loophole that marks the boundary of what continuous-space defenses can guarantee. Experiments on a $3 \times 5$ attack-defense matrix with bootstrap confidence intervals, Bonferroni-corrected hypothesis tests, and Clopper-Pearson validation ($n=1{,}000$) confirm: composite defenses achieve TPR $= 1.00$, FPR $= 0.00$ across all attacks, while synonym substitution evades detection at $\Delta$ ASR-R $\approx 0$, exposing a gap existing embedding-based defenses cannot close.
author: Ishrith Gowda (University of California, Berkeley)

title: Free Energy-Driven Reinforcement Learning with Adaptive Advantage Shaping for Unsupervised Reasoning in LLMs
content:
arXiv:2605.04065v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Unsupervised reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a promising paradigm for enabling self-improvement in large language models (LLMs). However, existing unsupervised RL-based methods often lack the capacity to adapt to the model's evolving reasoning capabilities during training. Therefore, these methods can misdirect policy optimization in the absence of ground-truth supervision. To address this issue, we introduce FREIA, a novel RL-based algorithm built on two key innovations: (1) Free Energy-Driven Reward (FER) adapts rewards to balance consensus and exploration based on the Free Energy Principle. (2) Adaptive Advantage Shaping (AAS) adaptively adjusts learning signals based on the statistical characteristics of sampled rewards. Empirical evaluations on nine datasets across three reasoning tasks showcase that FREIA outperforms other unsupervised RL-based baselines. Notably, in mathematical reasoning tasks, FREIA surpasses other methods by an average of 0.5 to 3.5 points in Pass@1 using the DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B model.
author: Yiming Huang, Zhenbo Shi, Xin-Cheng Wen, Jichuan Zeng, Cuiyun Gao, Peiyi Han, Chuanyi Liu

title: Adapt to Thrive! Adaptive Power-Mean Policy Optimization for Improved LLM Reasoning
content:
arXiv:2605.04066v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) is an essential paradigm that enhances the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, existing methods typically rely on static policy optimization schemes that misalign with the model's evolving reasoning capabilities. To address this issue, we propose Adaptive Power-Mean Policy Optimization (APMPO), which comprises two main innovations: Power-Mean Policy Optimization (PMPO) and Feedback-Adaptive Clipping (FAC). Specifically, PMPO introduces a generalized power-mean objective. This enables the model to adaptively transition from the signal-amplifying behavior of the arithmetic mean to the consistency-enforcing behavior of the geometric mean. FAC adaptively adjusts clipping bounds based on real-time reward statistics to overcome the limitations of static mechanisms. Capitalizing on these innovations, APMPO improves learning dynamics and reasoning performance. Extensive experiments on nine datasets across three reasoning tasks showcase the superiority of APMPO over state-of-the-art RLVR-based baselines. For instance, APMPO boosts the average Pass@1 score on mathematical reasoning benchmarks by 3.0 points compared to GRPO when using Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct.
author: Yiming Huang, Zhenbo Shi, Shuzheng Gao, Cuiyun Gao, Peiyi Han, Chuanyi Liu

title: Contextual Memory-Enhanced Source Coding for Low-SNR Communications
content:
arXiv:2605.04400v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: While Separate Source-Channel Coding (SSCC) retains the practical benefits of modular system design, its effectiveness in noisy text transmission is fundamentally constrained by the fragility of autoregressive source decoding. In low-SNR regimes, even a small number of residual bit errors after channel decoding may derail the subsequent lossless reconstruction process, especially when Arithmetic Coding (AC) relies on Large Language Model (LLM)-based probability estimation. Existing remedies either strengthen channel decoding based solely on channel observations or introduce contextual information only at the receiver for post-hoc correction, yet neither fully addresses the fragility of source probability modeling under residual channel errors. To this end, this paper proposes a Memory-Augmented Source Coding (MASC) scheme for robust SSCC-based transmission. Rather than treating context as external side information, MASC internalizes contextual patterns into a source model shared by both the transmitter-side source encoder and the receiver-side source decoder. Specifically, MASC employs a shared Parameterized Contextual Memory (PCM) to encode multi-order $n$-gram patterns, and further introduces a Mixture-of-Memory-Experts Router (MMER) to perform sparse, hidden-state-dependent routing over memory experts during autoregressive source modeling. By adaptively activating only the most relevant memories at each coding step, MASC refines source probability estimation, shortens average codelength, and mitigates the sensitivity of source decoding to residual channel errors. Extensive experiments over Rayleigh fading and AWGN channels demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed scheme compared with state-of-the-art methods.
author: Ziqiong Wang, Rongpeng Li

title: Predictive and Prescriptive AI toward Optimizing Wildfire Suppression
content:
arXiv:2605.04510v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Intense wildfire seasons require critical prioritization decisions to allocate scarce suppression resources over a dispersed geographical area. This paper develops a predictive and prescriptive approach to jointly optimize crew assignments and wildfire suppression. The problem features a discrete resource-allocation structure with endogenous wildfire demand and non-linear wildfire dynamics. We formulate an integer optimization model with crew assignments on a time-space-rest network, wildfire dynamics on a time-state network, and linking constraints between them. We develop a two-sided branch-and-price-and-cut algorithm based on: (i) a two-sided column generation scheme that generates fire suppression plans and crew routes iteratively; (ii) a new family of cuts exploiting the knapsack structure of the linking constraints; and (iii) novel branching rules to accommodate non-linear wildfire dynamics. We also propose a data-driven double machine learning approach to estimate wildfire spread as a function of covariate information and suppression efforts, mitigating observed confounding between historical crew assignments and wildfire growth. Extensive computational experiments show that the optimization algorithm scales to otherwise intractable real-world instances; and that the methodology can enhance suppression effectiveness in practice, resulting in significant reductions in area burned over a wildfire season and guiding resource sharing across wildfire jurisdictions.
author: Leonard Boussioux, Alexandre Jacquillat, Ryne Reger, Jacob Wachspress

title: Rethinking Convolutional Networks for Attribute-Aware Sequential Recommendation
content:
arXiv:2605.04723v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Attribute-aware sequential recommendation entails predicting the next item a user will interact with based on a chronologically ordered history of past interactions, enriched with item attributes. Existing methods typically leverage self-attention mechanisms to aggregate the entire sequence into a unified representation used for next-item prediction. While effective, these models often suffer from high computational complexity and memory consumption, limiting their ability to process long user histories. This constraint restricts the model's capacity to fully capture long-term user preferences. In some scenarios, modeling item interactions purely through attention may also not be the most effective approach to extract sequential patterns. In this work, we propose ConvRec, an alternative method with linear computational and memory complexity that employs convolutional layers in a hierarchical, down-scaled fashion to generate compact, yet expressive sequence representations. To further enhance the model's ability to capture diverse sequential patterns, each layer aggregates the neighboring items gradually to reach a comprehensive sequence representation. Extensive experiments on four real-world datasets demonstrate that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art sequential recommendation models, highlighting the potential of convolution-based architectures for efficient and effective sequence modeling in recommendation systems. Our implementation code and datasets are available here https://github.com/ismll-research/ConvRec.
author: Shereen Elsayed, Ngoc Son Le, Ahmed Rashed, Lars Schmidt-Thieme

title: Rethinking Local Learning: A Cheaper and Faster Recipe for LLM Post-Training
content:
arXiv:2605.04913v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: LLM post-training typically propagates task gradients through the full depth of the model. Although this end-to-end structure is simple and general, it couples task adaptation to full-depth activation storage, long-range backward dependencies and direct task-gradient access to pretrained representations. We argue that this full-depth backward coupling can be unnecessarily expensive and intrusive, particularly when post-training supervision is much narrower than pre-training. To this end, we propose \textbf{LoPT}: Local-Learning Post-Training, a simple post-training strategy that makes gradient reach an explicit design choice. LoPT places a single gradient boundary at the transformer midpoint: the second-half block learns from the task objective, while the first-half block is updated by a lightweight feature-reconstruction objective to preserve useful representations and maintain interface compatibility. LoPT shortens the task-induced backward path while limiting direct interference from narrow task gradients on early-layer representations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LoPT achieves competitive performance with lower memory cost, higher training efficiency and better retention of pretrained capabilities. Our code is available at: https://github.com/HumyuShi/LoPT
author: Hengyu Shi, Tianyang Han, Peizhe Wang, Zhiling Wang, Xu Yang, Junhao Su

title: Neural Discovery of Strichartz Extremizers
content:
arXiv:2605.04918v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Strichartz inequalities are a cornerstone of the modern theory of dispersive PDEs, but their extremizers are known explicitly only in a handful of sharp cases. The non-convexity of the underlying functional makes the problem hard, and to our knowledge no systematic numerical attack has been attempted. We propose a simple neural-network-based pipeline that searches for extremizers as critical points of the Strichartz ratio, and apply it in three settings. First, on the Schr\"odinger group we recover the Gaussian extremizers of Foschi and Hundertmark--Zharnitsky in dimensions $d=1,2$ to within $10^{-3}$ relative error, with no analytical prior. Second, on $59$ further admissible pairs in $d=1$ where the answer is conjectural, the method consistently finds Gaussians, supporting the conjecture that Gaussians are the universal extremizers in the admissible range. Third, on the critical Airy--Strichartz inequality at $\gamma=1/q$, where existence is open, the optimization does not converge to any $L^2$ profile: instead, the iterates organize themselves as mKdV breathers $B(0,\cdot;\alpha,1,0,0)$ with growing internal frequency $\alpha$, and the discovered ratio approaches the Frank--Sabin universal lower bound $\widetilde A_{q,r}$ from below with a power-law gap $\sim\alpha^{-0.9}$. We confirm the same picture with an independent Hermite-basis ansatz. We propose a precise conjecture: the supremum equals $\widetilde A_{q,r}$ and is approached, but not attained, along the breather family. The pipeline thus serves both as a validator on known cases and as a discovery tool when no extremizer exists.
author: Nicol\'as Valenzuela, Ricardo Freire, Claudio Mu\~noz

title: Learning the chemical language of natural products
content:
Nature Machine Intelligence, Published online: 07 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s42256-026-01241-9

A promising foundation model is developed for a range of downstream applications in natural product mining.
author: Xu Guo, Celia M. Rava, Allison S. Walker

title: Platonic representation of foundation machine learning interatomic potentials
content:
Nature Machine Intelligence, Published online: 07 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s42256-026-01235-7

Li and Walsh show that a unified ‘platonic’ geometry emerges across the representations of independent foundation models for learning interatomic potentials. The observation enables cross-model comparison, embedding arithmetic and ground-truth-free diagnostics.
author: Zhenzhu Li, Aron Walsh

title: SRDiff: A Cross-Modal Diffusion Model for Satellite-to-Radar Translation in Precipitation Nowcasting
content: Satellite-to-radar translation is a critical yet underexplored task in modern weather forecasting. While satellites provide near-global coverage and high-frequency updates, their multichannel radiance data is difficult to interpret directly by forecasters or end users, and not readily compatible with existing radar-based forecasting models. By converting satellite observations into radar-equivalent representations, we can: 1) extend radar-like availability to regions without ground-based radar coverage; 2) provide a more compact and operationally meaningful representation of storms; and 3) enable the reuse of a large ecosystem of radar-based nowcasting models without retraining. Despite its importance, methods tailored for this task remain limited, and the performance of general-purpose generative models has not been systematically benchmarked. To fill this gap, we propose SRDiff, a cross-modal, sequence-aware diffusion model specifically designed for satellite-to-radar translation. SRDiff employs a cross-modal conditional adapter (CMCA) to align heterogeneous satellite channels with radar reflectivity, and a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) backbone to capture sequential dependencies, producing stable and coherent radar predictions. Extensive experiments on the SEVIR and Sat2Rdr datasets show that SRDiff outperforms existing deterministic and diffusion-based methods on the satellite-to-radar translation task across all key metrics. As a downstream application, we integrate SRDiff with off-the-shelf radar-based nowcasting models, demonstrating three key advantages: 1) operational efficiency, since no new training is required; 2) strong forecasting accuracy, often surpassing models trained from scratch; and 3) practical validation that effective satellite-to-radar translation directly improves downstream nowcasting. The code is available at https://github.com/42xingxing/SRDiff
author: You Qin, Jinming Cao, Ting Wang, Yifang Yin, Li Li, Shili Xiang, Ying Zhang, Roger Zimmermann

title: Retrieval of Ocean Surface and Atmospheric Parameters From K- and Ka-Band Data Using a Physics-Informed Coupled Neural Network
content: Sea surface temperature (SST), sea surface wind speed (SSW), and total column water vapor (WV) are critical parameters for understanding ocean–atmosphere interactions. While conventional microwave retrieval methods typically rely on C- and X-band channels, this study proposes a physics-informed coupled neural network (PIC-NN) designed to jointly retrieve all three parameters using only K- and Ka-band brightness temperatures (TBs). The model incorporates two key components: bidirectional coupling connections, which explicitly encode known physical interactions among SST, SSW, and WV, and an adaptive scaling factor loss function that balances the multitask learning process. Validated against ERA5 reanalysis data and multiple in situ datasets, PIC-NN achieves SST retrieval accuracy comparable to that of the operational Advanced Microwave Scanning Radiometer 2 (AMSR-2) product (which uses lower frequencies). Moreover, it significantly outperforms AMSR-2 in retrieving SSW and WV, achieving standard deviation (STD) reductions of 19.0% and 23.7%, respectively. Ablation studies confirm the contribution of each model component, and comparisons with traditional methods highlight the importance of modeling nonlinearity and physical coupling. Furthermore, validated on an in-orbit high-frequency (HF) payload, this work demonstrates the robust generalization of multiparameter retrievals using HF bands alone. Under potential constraints of low-frequency spectrum scarcity or specific payload designs restricted to high frequencies, this approach provides a viable solution for accurately extracting key ocean–atmosphere parameters.
author: Peng Mao, Xiaobin Yin, Ning Wang, Yan Li, Qing Xu, Qingtao Song, Xingwei Jiang

title: Water Depth Inversion by Fusion of Active and Passive Remote Sensing Imagery With Waves Under the Super Learner–Neural ODE Framework
content: Most active and passive remote sensing imagery acquired over marine areas contains wave textures, presenting both challenges and opportunities for water depth retrieval. Although wave phenomena hinder water depth estimation via optical radiative transfer in passive remote sensing, they form the foundation of bathymetric methods based on wave texture analysis. The synergistic use of active and passive imagery with wave textures helps to mitigate these adverse effects in certain inversion approaches. By integrating the inversion results from both data types—accounting for their distinct imaging mechanisms and depth detection theories—their complementary advantages can be effectively combined. To this end, a new fusion method based on a super learner–neural ODE (SL-NODE) ensemble is proposed. This two-stage framework implements a decision-level fusion strategy: multiple classical machine learning models act as base learners in the first stage, and the neural ordinary differential equation (Neural ODE) serves as the metalearner in the second. Outputs from the first-stage models are combined via cross validation and subsequently refined by the neural ODE to produce the final water depth estimates. In the range of 0–40-m depth range, the fused results exhibit reductions of over 17% in mean relative error (MRE) and more than 3 m in mean absolute error (MAE) compared to those from individual inversion methods. Moreover, the proposed ensemble achieves superior fusion accuracy to any single-classical machine learning model. The integration of multisource data and diverse inversion techniques significantly enhances the accuracy and utility of water depth retrieval from imagery with wave phenomena.
author: Aijun Cui, Yi Ma, Jingyu Zhang, Ruifu Wang

title: Physics-Constrained Deep Neural Networks for Short-Term 3-D Flow Field Prediction Using Coastal Acoustic Tomography
content: Accurate prediction of 3-D ocean flow fields is essential for understanding small-scale dynamic processes, improving environmental monitoring, and enhancing the performance of underwater observation and operation systems. However, predictive modeling using coastal acoustic tomography (CAT) data remains relatively unexplored. This study proposes a novel physics-constrained (PC) deep learning framework, termed PC-long short-term memory (LSTM)- multilayer perceptron (MLP), to achieve the short-term prediction of 3-D ocean flow fields from CAT observations. The framework consists of three integrated components: an LSTM network for capturing temporal dependencies in range-averaged currents (RACs), an MLP for nonlinear mapping to spatial Fourier coefficients, and a PC module that imposes a horizontal incompressibility constraint to enhance the accuracy of the predicted 3-D flow fields. Experimental results on real CAT data demonstrate that the proposed PC-LSTM-MLP framework effectively captures complex spatiotemporal dynamics and provides accurate predictions up to 25 min ahead. The model achieves an average mean absolute error (MAE) of 0.0241 m/s and an average root mean square error (RMSE) of 0.0269 m/s in 3-D flow field prediction. This research shifts CAT analysis from traditional inversion methods to intelligent, prediction-oriented modeling, underscoring the significant potential of deep learning in ocean flow field prediction.
author: Xiaofei Zhang, Shijie Xu, Guangming Li, Chee-Meng Chew, Xinyi Xie, Haocai Huang

title: UnderwaterDG: Domain Generalized Semantic Segmentation in Underwater Scenes via Decoupling Visibility and Appearance
content: Domain generalized semantic segmentation (DGSS) has made significant progress in urban scenes but remains unexplored in marine environments. Underwater images present diverse appearance and visibility variations caused by water quality, lighting, and habitat conditions, which significantly differ from those in urban scenes, making urban DGSS methods less effective in such environments. To fill this gap, we formally investigate DGSS for underwater scenes, termed UnderwaterDG, and propose a novel method focusing on reducing visibility and appearance domain shift. Specifically, we first propose a visibility–appearance decoupling modeling (VADM) framework that integrates the atmospheric scattering model (ASM) with adaptive instance normalization (AdaIN), enabling the disentanglement of underwater images into appearance and visibility components. Second, we propose a decoupled consistency loss (DCL) that enforces prediction consistency under variations in decoupled appearance and visibility, facilitating the learning of appearance- and visibility-invariant representations. Third, we propose a curriculum-guided style-masking perturbation (CSMP) strategy, which enhances generalization by dynamically modulating the difficulty of the disentanglement process. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first DGSS framework tailored for underwater environments, advancing semantic understanding in open and dynamic marine settings. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and generalization capability of the proposed approach across diverse underwater datasets.
author: Yuhang Zhang, Yi Tang, Shiyu Deng, Zhengyu Zhang, Xin Lin, Guoguang Hua

title: Model-Based Tensor Decompositions for Geophysical Parameter Retrieval From Multidimensional SAR Data
content: Tensor decompositions are mathematical methods for joint analysis and information extraction from multidimensional data. This work combines model-free algebraic tensor decompositions with physical synthetic aperture radar (SAR) models and introduces a novel framework for geophysical parameter retrieval from multidimensional SAR tensors. To demonstrate parameter retrieval, we combine information from polarimetric, temporal, and spatial data dimensions and invert soil moisture over vegetated agricultural areas. We formulate parameter inversion as a constrained optimization problem and retrieve the parameters by fitting the physical model to the observed SAR tensor. Compared to methods that operate on matrices, multidimensional tensors offer a larger observation space, reduce inversion ambiguities, and allow inversion of more complex models that cover a larger range of field conditions and crop types. The proposed method represents the signal as a sum of three components, separates the surface, dihedral, and volume signal contributions, and allows estimation of crop-specific volume scattering polarimetric signatures. With the increasing availability of multidimensional SAR data, model-based tensor decompositions gain relevance and provide a flexible, extensible, and explainable way to jointly analyze the information across multiple data dimensions.
author: Nikita Basargin, Alberto Alonso-González, Irena Hajnsek

title: LS-DSPerceiver: A Dual-Domain Feature Encoding and Scale-Aware Perceiver for Robust Multiscenario Landslide Extraction
content: Landslides, as a typical type of sudden geological hazards, pose serious threats to human lives and infrastructure. Although high-resolution remote sensing imagery provides a solid data foundation for landslide detection, existing methods still face significant challenges in fine-grained extraction across diverse scenarios due to substantial scale variations, complex semantic interference, and ambiguous structural boundaries. To address these challenges, we propose a dual-domain feature encoding and scale-aware perceiver (LS-DSPerceiver). Specifically, an adaptive frequency-domain transformation (AFDT) together with an asymmetric cross-domain encoder is introduced to explicitly decouple semantic modeling from structural representation, thereby mitigating the mutual interference between semantic and structural features. In addition, a multiscale-in-multiscale staircase scale-aware aggregation (MMSS) strategy is designed, in which multilevel aggregation (MLA) performs progressive fusion across adjacent hierarchies, while multidirectional cascaded structural enhancement (MDCS) is employed within each hierarchy to enhance the representation of elongated landslide structures. This design enables dual multiscale modeling across and within hierarchical levels and further reinforces the semantic consistency required for landslide discrimination. Furthermore, a boundary-aware module (BAM) is employed in the decoding stage to refine structural details. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance on the GVLM, Bijie, Landslide4Sense, and CAS datasets, with intersection over union (IoU) improvements of 1.69%, 1.73%, 1.56%, and 1.62%, respectively. Moreover, the method attains competitive performance using only 10% of the target-domain data, highlighting its strong generalization capability and cross-region adaptability.
author: Min Chen, Jinbo Zhang, Caiyi Li, Wei Guo, Tong Fang, Yitao Zhao, Xuming Ge, Bo Xu, Han Hu, Qing Zhu

title: Spectral Representation-Enhanced Multitask SAM for Cropland Parcel Delineation
content: Accurate cropland parcel delineation (CLPD) from remote sensing imagery is crucial for fine-scale agricultural monitoring. The recent segment anything model (SAM), as a foundational vision model, demonstrates remarkable generalization capabilities, presenting significant potential for CLPD. However, SAM’s design for RGB natural imagery limits its ability to model multispectral information effectively. To address these limitations, we propose a spectrally enhanced multitask segmentation framework built upon SAM for CLPD. Our approach enhances SAM in two key aspects: first, to better leverage multispectral data, we introduce a decoupled learning strategy designed to improve SAM’s spectral representation. It is implemented through two components: learnable channel tokens (CTs) encoding band-specific spectral identities and a lightweight channel-attention module modeling interband relationships. Second, to enhance decoding robustness under high intraparcel spectral variability, we introduce a feature-structure regularization loss that enforces intraclass compactness and interclass separation, along with an auxiliary instance-guided decoding branch that captures fine-grained subparcel patterns. These patterns are integrated into the main segmentation stream via a token-based task interaction module. Extensive experiments on two public datasets demonstrate consistent improvements of the proposed method. On the Sen4AgriNet dataset, it achieves a pixel-level  $F1$ -score of 89.36% and reduces the object-level global total classification error (GTC) by 0.97% compared with existing methods. Ablation studies further validate the contribution of each component and confirm the effectiveness of our decoupling strategy. The source code will be made available at: https://github.com/halfhalfer/MSSAM
author: Aoran Hu, Siyao Cheng, Ran Meng, Zeyuan Li, Jie Liu

title: DHCNet: Dynamic Hierarchical Collaborative Learning for Change Detection in High-Resolution Remote Sensing Images
content: High-resolution remote sensing (HRRS) image change detection (CD) can effectively monitor dynamic changes of the Earth’s surface. In recent years, deep learning-based remote sensing image CD (RSCD) methods have developed rapidly. However, they still have shortcomings in spatio-temporal feature interaction of images, multiscale feature enhancement (MSFE), and multilevel information fusion. Therefore, this article proposes a dynamic hierarchical collaborative learning method (DHCNet) for accurate RSCD. First, a dynamic hierarchical collaborative interaction (DHCI) module is designed. It improves the richness of bi-temporal multigranularity features through layer-differentiated feature exchange and cross-temporal collaborative attention mechanism. Second, an MSFE module is constructed to enhance DHCNet’s ability to represent the texture and semantic information of changed ground objects. Furthermore, a multilevel feature reconstruction (MLFR) module is proposed. It deeply fuses the enhanced multilevel features to reconstruct key change information and improve the model’s CD performance. Experimental results on the LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, CDD, Real-Wuhan, and S1GFloods datasets show that DHCNet’s CD outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, with  $F1$ -scores reaching 91.55%, 95.73%, 96.40%, 75.63%, and 96.36%, respectively. Comparative and ablation experiments confirm that DHCNet has significant advantages in CD of complex scenarios and fine-grained boundary representation. Our source codes are available at https://github.com/WBJIIKe/DHCNet.
author: Weilong Chen, Qing Ding, Fengyan Wang, Mingchang Wang, Kaiyuan Sun

title: FLINet: Fuzzy Logic for Infrared Small-Target Detection
content: Infrared small-target detection (IRSTD) remains challenging due to weak target responses, cluttered backgrounds, and large variations in target appearance. In this article, we propose FLINet, a fuzzy-logic-guided network that introduces a fuzzy set perspective for uncertainty-aware feature fusion in infrared small-target detection. FLINet follows a U-shaped encoder–decoder architecture and incorporates two key modules: a global–local fuzzy (GLF) computation module and a difference-aware feature (DAF) fusion module. GLF performs dual-branch fuzzy reasoning at both local-window and global-image levels to model feature uncertainty during cross-level fusion. Specifically, the local branch enhances contrast-sensitive target cues via adaptive membership-based amplification, while the global branch suppresses clutter-dominant responses through global statistical normalization, jointly improving target-background separation and morphology preservation. DAF further strengthens cross-level integration by exploiting interlevel discrepancy maps to guide a self-attention-based fusion, prioritizing complementary fine-grained details from shallow layers and semantic cues from deep layers to reduce small-target information loss. Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets (NUDT-SIRST, NUAA-SIRST, and IRSTD-1K) demonstrate that FLINet consistently achieves superior performance across multiple evaluation metrics. Our code is available at https://github.com/P-ZYang/FLINet
author: Yang Zhang, Meibin Qi, Kunyuan Li, Xiaohong Li, Shuo Zhuang

title: An Automatic Identification Method for Linear Discontinuities on High-Steep Slopes Based on Thermal Infrared Imaging: A Case Study in the Jinsha River Suture Zone
content: Discontinuities are critical factors that influence slope stability. While planar discontinuities can be reliably identified, linear discontinuities remain challenging to identify because of their concealed nature and limited surface exposure. This study introduces an improved neighborhood search algorithm that utilizes thermal infrared (TIR) images to identify linear discontinuities. The method identifies discontinuities based on their thermal characteristics, using temperature differences between intact rock and discontinuities. To evaluate the effectiveness of the algorithm, a typical high-steep rock slope in the upper Jinsha River region was selected. High-resolution visible and TIR images were acquired using an autonomous aerial vehicle (UAV), and the method was applied to identify and validate discontinuities. Comparison with the results of manual identification indicated a positive correlation between discontinuity scale and identification rate. For linear discontinuities exceeding 10 m in trace length, the identification rate reached 70.5%. The method also demonstrated potential advantages in capturing temperature difference boundaries caused by hot air movement and in delineating unstable areas, as well as open discontinuities and rockfalls. This research provides a new idea for the automatic identification of linear discontinuities in complex slope conditions and offers valuable insights for rock mass engineering evaluation.
author: Wen Zhang, Qing Liu, Jia Wang, Han Yin, Yaoyao Wang, Junqi Chen, Xiaohan Zhao

title: Fine-Scale Structure Reconstruction of Weather Radar Echoes via Blind Super-Resolution
content: Due to the unavailability of real high-resolution (HR) radar data, most super-resolution (SR) methods for weather radar rely on low-resolution (LR) samples synthesized using fixed and simplified degradation schemes. However, such excessively simplified schemes are inadequate to capture the complex degradations introduced by hardware and acquisition constraints. As a result, HR-LR training sets generated in this manner deviate from real-world observations, leading to poor generalization: models often perform well on synthetic test sets but exhibit pronounced performance drops when applied to operational radar observations, thereby substantially limiting practical applicability. These shortcomings underscore the necessity of introducing blind SR (BSR) for radar echo reconstruction. BSR aims to recover HR radar fields from LR inputs under unknown degradations during reconstruction. To achieve this, diverse degradations are incorporated during training to reduce the mismatch between synthetic data and real-world radar observations, thereby improving robustness and generalization. Despite its considerable potential, BSR has seen limited exploration and application in weather radar. To increase radar-specific degradation diversity, a radar-oriented degradation bank (RODBank) is constructed by incorporating key measurement characteristics, enabling more realistic emulation of measurement-induced degradations and improving training set realism. Using this degradation bank, the SR model is trained in a supervised manner with synthetic HR-LR pairs generated from HR tiles, while reconstruction is performed without specifying an explicit degradation model or degradation parameters. An enhanced GAN-based SR framework (SRGAN), termed dual-attention residual SR (DARSR), is further introduced to strengthen fine-scale structure recovery for weather radar reflectivity factor under unknown degradations. Results show that DARSR outperforms the compared methods under unknown degradations and exhibits robust performance across diverse degradation scenarios. In particular, cross-platform comparisons using S-band observations, which were not included in the X-band-only training data, suggest that the proposed model exhibits encouraging transferability beyond the training platform and demonstrates its potential for resolution-limited radar systems to recover fine-scale features.
author: Rui Li, Shiqian Li, Jianxin He, Shunxian Tang, Zhao Shi

title: Generalized Iterative Sparse Maximum Likelihood Algorithm for the Detection of Buried Targets
content: This article presents a generalized iterative sparse maximum likelihood algorithm (GSMLA) for buried-target detection using ground penetrating radar (GPR). The proposed approach extends the classical SMLA family (SMLA-0, SMLA-1, and SMLA-2) while addressing their individual limitations. Although SMLA-0 achieves strong sparsity and effectively suppresses smearing, it struggles to recover scatterers under low-SNR conditions. SMLA-1 improves detectability but remains affected by smearing, outliers, and limited resolution. SMLA-2 provides a more balanced tradeoff, yet it cannot simultaneously preserve sparsity and high resolution while suppressing outliers and smearing artifacts. In contrast, GSMLA achieves sparse, high-resolution reconstructions with enhanced robustness to noise and improved suppression of outliers and smearing. An adaptive strategy is introduced to automatically select the regularization parameter  $\alpha $  using negative log-likelihood evaluation, allowing GSMLA to self-adapt to different acquisition scenarios without manual tuning. Moreover, the proposed method incorporates a ray-tracing-based permittivity compensation, which accounts for the influence of the homogeneous medium on wave propagation, further improving the accuracy of scatterer localization. The effectiveness of GSMLA is demonstrated using both simulated and measured GPR data, where it consistently delivers superior imaging performance compared with existing SMLA variants.
author: Lekhmissi Harkati

title: Polarimetric Calibration for Long Wavelength Spaceborne SAR Based on a Polarimetrically Active Radar Calibrator
content: For long wavelength spaceborne SAR, Faraday rotation (FR), system errors (crosstalk and channel imbalance) and system noise all combine to affect the accuracy of polarimetric measurements. This is of particular concern for the European Space Agency (ESA) P-band SAR BIOMASS mission. Measurements from a polarimetric active radar calibrator (PARC) during the commissioning phase and periodically through the mission play a key role in estimating the system errors for BIOMASS. An algorithm to perform such estimation was given in Chen et al. (2011), but it uses an inadequate model for errors in the PARC and only yields sufficiently accurate results if a very accurate independent estimate of FR is available. The modified analytic algorithm proposed here gives significantly better performance than the Chen et al. (2011) algorithm and can be slightly improved when coupled with numerical optimization, as demonstrated by simulation. A first-order analysis of the modified estimator yields simple expressions for the mean and variance of the estimated FR and system errors, and indicates that these are unbiased, except for the real parts of two of the crosstalk terms. Explicit expressions for these biases are derived, which allows their correction. This analysis also partitions the overall error between its different sources, allowing their relative importance to be quantified. Simulation indicates that the first-order analysis yields very accurate results.
author: Shaun Quegan, Mark R. Lomas

title: Key-Feature-Assisted Joint Dynamic Estimation and 3-D Reconstruction for Spaceborne ISAR Imaging of Fixed-Axis Spinning Target
content: Space situational awareness (SSA) faces critical challenges in characterizing the dynamics and structures of fixed-axis spinning space objects using spaceborne inverse synthetic aperture radar (ISAR). The performance of existing methods depends on high-quality 2-D imaging and precise feature extraction, which suffer from 3-D spatial migration and phase errors under complex relative motion. To address the aforementioned challenges, we propose a joint dynamic estimation and 3-D reconstruction framework based on key-feature-assisted Doppler coefficients analysis. After modeling the complex relative motion of the imaging system and analyzing the imaging characteristics of the satellite target, we establish an overdetermined equation that describes the connections between Doppler coefficients and target parameters. Our framework is as follows. First, we introduce a hierarchical multiorder Doppler coefficients estimation method combining coarse estimation and stabilized Doppler estimation via 2-D alternating direction method of multipliers (SDE-2-D-ADMMs). Second, we leverage spatial distribution characteristics of Doppler coefficients to extract planar features without image segmentation. Third, we employ a magnitude-assisted Kalman filter (MA-KF) for robust scatter-point association and solve overdetermined equations via an optimization algorithm for rapid rotational parameter estimation and 3-D surface reconstruction. Experimental validation across multiple spin states and orbital conditions demonstrates superior performance in rotational velocity estimation and 3-D surface reconstruction.
author: Yichen Zhou, Yong Wang, Wenjun Ding, Junqiu Zhang

title: Multilayer Ionosphere Correction in Low-Frequency Interferometric SAR: Modeling, Inversion, and Application to BIOMASS
content: Early analysis of BIOMASS interferometric data acquired during the commissioning phase at Boreal and Antarctic latitudes revealed the presence of strong ionospheric disturbances that cause severe coherence losses. This phenomenon was investigated by studying the variations of the interferometric phase with respect to the squint angle. The observed phase variations turned out to be consistent with the assumption of a complex vertical structure of the ionospheric phase, characterized by fast azimuth variations occurring at km or sub-km scale at multiple layers between the ground and the satellite orbit. To the best of our knowledge, this phenomenon has never been considered in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) literature. This article is therefore intended to present and discuss the results of our investigation on suitable correction methods needed to guarantee the generation of accurate geophysical products from BIOMASS interferometric data. The starting point of our analysis is a multilayered model of the ionospheric phase formulated through multisquint interferometry (MS-InSAR), that is, by varying the radar line of sight (LoS) across different squint angles. The model is analyzed from a theoretical perspective to discuss the sensitivity of BIOMASS to ionospheric layering. Next, an inversion procedure is presented to estimate the ionospheric phase at an arbitrary number of layers directly from the complex coherence. The third element of our research consists of the formulation of correction methods to restore data quality given the estimated single- or multilayer ionospheric phase. All aspects of the research are consistently supported by analysis of BIOMASS interferometric data acquired at different latitudes and affected by mild to very strong ionospheric disturbances. The capability of the developed methods to correct BIOMASS interferometric data was quantitatively evaluated by comparison against interferograms generated by data-based coregistration. In the case of strong ionospheric disturbances occurring at Boreal and Antarctic latitudes, the developed methods are observed to largely outperform data-based coregistration. Specifically, coherence magnitude over the Antarctica dataset was observed to be about 0.41 on average with data-based coregistration, and over 0.86 with the proposed multilayer correction.
author: Stefano Tebaldini, Francesco Salvaterra, Francesco Banda, Muriel Pinheiro

title: STF-FlowDiff: A Diffusion-Based SpatioTemporal Fusion Method With Optical Flow Guidance
content: Spatiotemporal fusion (STF) of remote sensing image is essential for dynamic monitoring and analysis of the Earth’s surface. By integrating multisource observations, it effectively mitigates the limitations of individual sensors in spatial resolution and temporal frequency. However, it remains challenging under complex land-cover changes and temporal misalignment. To solve this problem, a diffusion-based STF method with optical flow guidance, STF-FlowDiff, is proposed to exploit temporal dynamics. Dense optical flow is first estimated between the reference fine observation and a proxy fine image constructed from the coarse input, and the resulting motion field is incorporated as an explicit prior to guide diffusion-based STF, thereby improving temporal consistency. To enhance robustness, motion reliability is evaluated by a gradient-based flow confidence (GFC) module, and stable regions are selectively emphasized. In addition, motion features are injected into the diffusion encoder at corresponding resolutions by a multiscale flow injection (MFI) module, enabling effective modeling of hierarchical displacement patterns across scales. The effectiveness of the proposed method is validated through STF experiments on three real-world datasets. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms classical approaches and state-of-the-art STF algorithms, achieving superior fusion accuracy, reliable downstream task performance, and strong robustness with respect to model parameters.
author: Hui Shen, Hongjun Su, Xiaojuan Liu, Hui Yang, Qian Du

title: Spatially Varying Degradation Modeling and Pansharpening for Large-Scale Multispectral Images
content: Existing pansharpening methods mostly rely on globally uniform degradation modeling, such as applying a single MTF-matched image filter or a set of fixed spectral coefficients to the entire image. However, with increasing spatial resolution and coverage of satellite imagery, regional variations in sensor response and atmospheric conditions become non-negligible. These factors introduce spatially varying degradations that cannot be adequately captured by unified models and continuing to use globally uniform degradation models risks introducing spectral or spatial distortions. In this article, we propose an unsupervised pansharpening method based on spatially varying degradation modeling (SVDM). We first build pixel-wise degradation models for the multispectral image (MSI) and panchromatic image (PAN), where each pixel has its own model parameters. Then, we use a fully connected neural network called SVDMNet to efficiently represent them. The SVDMNet also stores the subtle deformation field between MSI and PAN, enabling precise image registration (IR) of the two images. After the training of SVDMNet, we embed it into the classical unsupervised pansharpening framework and freeze its parameters to guide the fusion process of deep pansharpening networks (PSNets). Finally, we construct spatially varying multiloss weights to balance the spatial and spectral loss functions based on the degradation modeling errors. Extensive experiments on various large-scale multispectral datasets demonstrate that the proposed SVDM method can accurately describe complex degradation processes of MSIs and PANs, showing superiority in the pansharpening task of large-scale MSIs and PANs. The code is available at https://github.com/codegaj/SVDM
author: Anjing Guo, Renwei Dian, Nan Wang, Shutao Li

title: A Supervised–Unsupervised Parallel Learning Pan-Sharpening Method Tailored for Spectrally Uncovered Bands
content: Pan-sharpening technology aims to fuse panchromatic and multi-spectral remote sensing images, so as to integrate their complementary advantages in the spatial and spectral domains. However, most of the existing pan-sharpening methods focus on the spatial enhancement of the red, green, blue (RGB), and near-infrared (NIR) bands, whose spectral ranges can be covered by the panchromatic imagery. In contrast, spectral bands with detection ranges and response intensities that are distinct from those of the panchromatic imagery usually exhibit degraded fusion performance. In this article, to address this issue, we propose a supervised–unsupervised dual-branch parallel learning pan-sharpening framework tailored for spectrally uncovered bands. Within each branch, the high-frequency texture features of the panchromatic imagery and the intrinsic spatial enhancement potential of the adjacent spectrally covered bands are leveraged, embedding a band self-guided attention (BSGA) mechanism to progressively infuse spatial information into the spectrally uncovered bands. In addition, a band-wise cumulative frequency constraint is imposed to ensure balanced fusion effects across all bands. Last but not least, the dual branches are interconnected through a cross-resolution-channel interactive learning (CRCIL) mechanism to balance the multi-scale performance. Extensive pan-sharpening experiments on WorldView-2 (WV2) and Landsat 8 images, plus hyperspectral (HS)-sharpening experiments on ZiYuan-1 02-D images, demonstrate that the proposed method can effectively enhance the spatial information for spectrally uncovered bands and achieve balanced fusion results over all spectral bands, presenting remarkable application potential in fusion scenarios involving complex and diverse spectral ranges.
author: Boxuan Zhang, Menghui Jiang, Huifang Li, Zhonghang Qiu, Dekun Lin, Huanfeng Shen

title: Joint Classification of Hyperspectral and LiDAR Data Using Multiscale Convolution and Cross-Attention-Guided Mamba
content: With the rapid development of deep learning for hyperspectral image (HSI)–light detection and ranging (LiDAR) land-cover classification, Mamba has shown strong potential in feature representation and generalization. However, insufficient semantic interaction among multimodal features and limited multiscale feature modeling still restrict classification performance. To address these challenges, we propose a novel multiscale convolution and cross-attention-guided Mamba (MCCGM) network for joint HSI–LiDAR classification. This framework fully exploits the complementary strengths of distinct architectural components. Specifically, we design a morphological-feature-integrated multiscale CNN (MMC) to extract spatial–spectral features from HSIs, elevation information from LiDAR, and their morphological features, thereby enabling precise characterization of local edges and texture details of ground objects. In parallel, the Mamba fusion encoder (MFE) efficiently models long-range spectral dependencies to promote deeper interaction between HSI spectral features and LiDAR-derived structural information. To further enhance semantic alignment across modalities, a CNN-enhanced cross-attention (CCA) mechanism is embedded into the Mamba block, where multiscale convolutions generate enriched queries, keys, and values to highlight modality-specific correlations. The resulting attention-refined tokens are subsequently processed by Mamba’s state-space modeling, which strengthens global dependency learning while preserving intermodality alignment. Extensive experiments conducted on four publicly available benchmark datasets demonstrate that MCCGM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art algorithms, achieving superior classification accuracy and detection performance. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/cupid6868/MCCGM
author: Le Sun, Xinyu Wang, Guoqing Zhang, Yadang Chen, Liang Xiao

title: Endmember Extraction Guided Transferable Adversarial Attack for Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: In recent years, adversarial attacks in hyperspectral image (HSI) classification have attracted increasing attention. However, most existing approaches rely on the loss or gradient information of a single classifier to optimize pixel-level adversarial perturbations, which undermines the reflectance consistency of hyperspectral data and limits the transferability of adversarial examples across different classifier architectures. These drawbacks compromise both the physical interpretability and the practical applicability of adversarial examples. To address these challenges, this article proposes an endmember extraction guided transferable adversarial attack (EEGTAA) framework for generating adversarial examples that preserve reflectance consistency while achieving the strong transferability. Specifically, EEGTAA first employs endmember extraction techniques to obtain the endmember composition of HSIs and introduces adversarial perturbations into the endmember matrix, thereby ensuring the physical plausibility of the generated samples. Subsequently, EEGTAA integrates the loss gradients from multiple heterogeneous classifiers to optimize perturbations on the endmember matrix, enhancing the threat to target classifiers with unknown architectures and parameters. Furthermore, a conflict-mitigation gradient optimization strategy is theoretically derived, which utilizes weighted loss gradients to correct the perturbation update direction within the hyperspherical neighborhood of the averaged gradient, alleviating the suboptimal optimization caused by divergent gradient directions across classifiers. Experimental results on benchmark HSI datasets demonstrate that EEGTAA not only produces adversarial examples with high-attack performance and reflectance consistency but also exhibits superior transferability across classifier architectures.
author: Kelin Dang, Hao Li, Mengxin Cao, Maoguo Gong, Yu Zhou, Yue Wu, Lining Xing

title: DIR-HSI: Degradation-Invariant Representation Learning for Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: In recent years, hyperspectral image (HSI) classification has achieved remarkable progress in fine-grained surface recognition and intelligent remote sensing analysis. However, real-world degradations—such as sensor noise, compression artifacts, and atmospheric interference—often introduce severe domain shifts, leading to degraded robustness and limited generalization of existing models. To address this challenge, we propose DIR-HSI, a degradation-invariant hyperspectral classification framework designed to enhance feature stability and discriminability under multidegradation conditions. The framework comprises two key modules: the degradation-invariant representation by Fourier-domain feature extraction (DIR-FFE) module, which leverages Fourier-domain feature decomposition to extract discriminative and degradation-insensitive frequency features, and the degradation-invariant representation contrastive enhancement (DIR-CE) module, which employs cross-degradation supervised contrastive (SupCon) learning to enforce consistency among same-class representations across different degradation conditions. Together, these components enable effective disentanglement of degradation-sensitive and stable factors, maintaining feature consistency and reinforcing class separability. The key advantage of DIR-HSI lies in its ability to jointly model degradation-invariant and discriminative representations, achieving robust and generalizable performance across complex degradation scenarios. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that DIR-HSI consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of accuracy, robustness, and generalization. The code will be available at https://github.com/yangboxiang1207/DIR-HSI
author: Boxiang Yang, Ning Chen, Yingbo Fan, Zexi Shi, Haoyuan Zhang, Hui Liu, Run Niu, Shanjun Mao

title: TM-MOE: A Degradation-Aware Transformer-Mamba MoE for Hyperspectral and Multispectral Image Fusion
content: With the increasing necessity for hyperspectral images (HSIs) with fine spatial–spectral details, the hyperspectral (HS) and multispectral (MS) image fusion scheme becomes a consequential mechanism to attain such images due to physical and expense constraints for the standard imaging instruments. With the rapid advancement of deep learning (DL) in the field of image processing, the ability to model long-range dependencies in both HS and MS images has become crucial for achieving superior performance in HS-MS image fusion. Transformer-based models address this challenge effectively through attention mechanisms, whereas state-space models offer a more efficient alternative in terms of both computation and memory usage. Therefore, we devise a degradation-aware transformer-Mamba MoE (TM-MOE) for HS-MS image fusion, which leverages the benefits of the cross-attention mechanism and Mamba to enhance the fusion consequences. The core part of the devised framework is the suggested degradation-aware cross transformer-Mamba (DACTM) model, which leverages the capacity of Mamba in processing long-range relationships with improved training efficiency and the performance of the transformer’s feature fusion for the observed pair. Moreover, the suggested Mamba module utilizes a 3-D scan to explore the correlation between pixels in an HSI, and it incorporates a mixture-of-experts (MoE) network to enrich model capability while maintaining effortless active parameter use. While the fusion process is accomplished on various scales, the novel feature accumulation and reconstruction block (FARB) devised reconstructs the final output from the fused features at different scales, gradually. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed TM-MOE. The code of this study will be available on https://github.com/Abdolraheemkhader/TM-MOE
author: Abdolraheem Khader, Guanyiman Fu, Jue Zhang, Jingxiang Yang, Jianfeng Lu, Liang Xiao

title: Scalable Neural Pushbroom Architectures for Real-Time Denoising of Hyperspectral Images Onboard Satellites
content: The next generation of Earth observation satellites will seek to deploy intelligent models directly onboard the payload to minimize the latency incurred by the transmission and processing chain of the ground segment, for time-critical applications. Designing neural architectures for onboard execution, particularly for satellite-based hyperspectral imagers, poses novel challenges due to the unique constraints of this environment and imaging system that are largely unexplored by the traditional computer vision literature. In this article, we show that this setting requires addressing three competing objectives, namely high-quality inference with low complexity, dynamic power scalability, and fault tolerance. We focus on the problem of hyperspectral image denoising, which is a critical task to enable effective downstream inference, and highlights the constraints of the onboard processing scenario. We propose a neural network design that addresses the three aforementioned objectives with several novel contributions. In particular, we propose a mixture of denoisers that can be resilient to radiation-induced faults, as well as allowing for time-varying power scaling. Moreover, each denoiser employs an innovative architecture where an image is processed line-by-line in a causal way, with a memory of past lines, to match the acquisition process of pushbroom hyperspectral sensors and greatly limit memory requirements. We show that the proposed architecture can run in real-time, i.e., process one line in the time it takes to acquire the next one, on low-power hardware and provide competitive denoising quality with respect to significantly more complex state-of-the-art models. We also show that the power scalability and fault tolerance objectives provide a design space with multiple trade-offs between those properties and denoising quality.
author: Ziyao Yi, Davide Piccinini, Diego Valsesia, Tiziano Bianchi, Enrico Magli

title: A Semantic Transformer for Self-Supervised Deep Clustering of Large Hyperspectral Images
content: Local window-based vision transformers (ViTs) have recently attracted significant attention in hyperspectral image (HSI) analysis for their balance between global modeling and efficiency. However, window-based self-attention in unsupervised HSI clustering still suffers from two key limitations: 1) implicitly restricting semantic correlations to a local window, overlooking fragmented and noncontiguous yet semantically homogeneous regions; and 2) using fixed grid windows, causing boundary mixing and weakening spectral–spatial discriminative representations. To overcome these issues, we propose a semantic transformer for self-supervised deep clustering (STSDC) of large HSIs, which couples a multiscale convolutional block (MSCB) with a semantic transformer under a unified self-supervised optimization module. Specifically, the MSCB branch employs parallel receptive fields with adaptive channel recalibration to preserve fine-grained structures and suppress spectral redundancy. The semantic transformer introduces superpixel-guided attention mechanisms, including semantic contiguous attention (SCA), which selects tokens from intrasuperpixel and adjacent superpixel to enhance spatial continuity, and semantic global attention (SGA), which aggregates long-range superpixel representations to associate spatially disjoint but semantically homogeneous regions. The self-supervised module aligns the embedding representations from the MSCB and the semantic transformer via a clustering objective. Extensive experiments on benchmark HSIs demonstrate that STSDC achieves superior clustering performance compared with state-of-the-art self-supervised methods for large HSIs.
author: Nan Huang, Cheng Li, Qichao Liu, Jinpeng Li, Pengfei Liu, Zhizhong Zheng, Liang Xiao

title: Boosting Multimodal Remote Sensing Image Classification With Transformer-Based Heterogeneously Salient Graph Representation
content: Data collected by different modalities can provide a wealth of complementary information, such as hyperspectral image (HSI) to offer rich spectral–spatial properties, synthetic aperture radar (SAR) to provide structural information about the Earth’s surface, and light detection and ranging (LiDAR) to cover altitude information about ground elevation. Therefore, a natural idea is to combine multimodal images for refined and accurate land-cover interpretation. Although many efforts have been made to achieve multi-source remote sensing (RS) image classification, there are still three issues, as follows: 1) indiscriminate feature representation without sufficiently considering modal heterogeneity; 2) abundant features and complex computations associated with modeling long-range dependencies; and 3) the overfitting phenomenon caused by sparsely labeled samples. To overcome the above barriers, a transformer-based heterogeneously salient graph representation (THSGR) approach is proposed in this article. First, a multimodal heterogeneous graph encoder is presented to encode distinctively non-Euclidean structural features from heterogeneous data. Then, a self-attention-free multi-convolutional modulator is designed for effective and efficient long-term dependency modeling. Finally, a mean forward strategy is developed in order to avoid overfitting. Based on the above structures, the proposed model is able to break through modal gaps to obtain a differentiated graph representation with competitive time cost, even for a small fraction of training samples. Experiments and analyses in three benchmark datasets with various state-of-the-art (SOTA) approaches show the performance of the proposed THSGR. The code will be available at https://github.com/jqyang22
author: Jiaqi Yang, Bo Du, Rong Liu, Zhu Mao, Liangpei Zhang

title: Infrared and Visible Ship Image Fusion Based on Adaptive Cross-Modal Feature Interaction and Multiscale Frequency-Domain Transformation
content: Infrared and visible image fusion serves as a pivotal technique for all-weather ship surveillance in maritime security applications, which can compensate for the inherent deficiencies of single-modal imaging under low-illumination and complex sea-state conditions. Nevertheless, existing fusion algorithms confront three core bottlenecks in coastal ship scenarios: first, insufficient robust feature representation capability against complex sea–sky backgrounds and multiscale ship targets; second, inefficient cross-modal feature interaction between infrared and visible modalities; third, weak scene adaptability arising from static weight allocation in multiscale frequency-domain fusion. To address these issues, this article proposes an infrared and visible ship image fusion algorithm (AMTFuse) based on adaptive cross-modal feature interaction and multiscale frequency-domain transformation, and constructs the IVSHIP dataset dedicated to ship image fusion. AMTFuse adopts an end-to-end framework and consists of three core modules: first, a contrastive learning-based feature enhancement network (CLFE) is developed, which mitigates small-target feature missing and insufficient modal discriminability in complex maritime scenarios via multiscale feature distillation paths, cross-modal contrastive learning, and enhanced spatial attention (ESA); second, an adaptive cross-modal feature interaction module (ACMF) is devised, which breaks through the limitations of static interaction and poor scene adaptability in existing cross-modal Transformers by virtue of modal-aware gating, multilevel feature alignment, and dynamic attention updating; finally, a multiscale dynamic frequency-domain fusion module (MSDF) is established, which implements multiscale frequency-domain decomposition and content-aware dynamic weight fusion at the deep feature level, remedying the defects of spatial–frequency decoupling and static weighting in conventional frequency-domain methods. Experimental results on the self-built IVSHIP dataset as well as public MSRS and TNO datasets demonstrate that AMTFuse significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art fusion algorithms in both quantitative metrics and visual effects. Furthermore, this article validates that the proposed algorithm can be applied to boost the performance of high-level computer vision tasks (i.e., object detection).
author: Limin Guo, Yuwu Wang, Xiaohai Zhou, Yue Wu, Guifu Yang

title: OFTNet: Omni-Feature and Texture-Aware Enhancement Learning Network for Continuous Remote Sensing Image Super-Resolution
content: Continuous-scale super-resolution (CSSR) for remote sensing images (RSIs) offers critical flexibility for diverse Earth observation tasks, overcoming the fixed-scale limitations of conventional SR methods. However, current CSSR methods are primarily tailored for natural images, failing to address the unique characteristics of remote sensing scenes. Unlike object-centric natural images with clear foregrounds, RSIs feature arbitrary orientations, vast scale variations, and dense repetitive patterns. First, standard hierarchical extractors in existing methods exhibit limited receptive fields, struggling to capture the global context required for large-scale land covers while preserving the fine-grained details of small targets. Second, the inherent spectral bias in implicit neural representations (INRs) exacerbates the loss of high-frequency information, causing severe over-smoothing in regions with intricate textures. To address these challenges, we propose an omni-feature and texture-aware enhancement learning network (OFTNet), a novel network specifically designed for continuous-scale RSI super-resolution (SR). Our method introduces a powerful omni-feature enhancement and refinement pipeline, comprising three key modules: an omni-feature enhancement module (OFEM) that synergistically models global spatial dependencies, interchannel correlations, and multiscale local patterns; a texture-aware enhancement module (TAEM) that explicitly preserves high-frequency information and models nonlocal spatial correlations; and a learnable weighting aggregation module (LWAM) for intelligent, context-aware feature aggregation. Extensive experiments on commonly used benchmark remote sensing datasets demonstrate that OFTNet achieves state-of-the-art performance across a wide range of in-distribution scales, while also showing robust and highly competitive performance on out-of-distribution (OOD) scales.
author: Shuang Chen, Shunzhou Wang, Yao Lu, Chun Li

title: FineCL: Exemplar-Free Continual Learning for Fine-Grained Remote Sensing Panoptic Segmentation
content: Continual learning (CL) enables the model to continually adapt to new data and tasks. However, the catastrophic forgetting and semantic drift lead to severe model degradation, especially in fine-grained segmentation tasks. Besides, replay-based methods bring extra storage burdens and raise privacy concerns. To address these challenges, we propose FineCL, an exemplar-free continual panoptic segmentation method. Concretely, FineCL proposes an anchoring contrastive distillation (ACD) method to preserve model stability by constraining the semantic consistency. To tackle semantic drift in fine-grained incremental learning (IL), FineCL proposes a dynamic memory bank (DMB) to enhance model plasticity via triplet prototype ranking loss for new knowledge integration. Without storing exemplar data, FineCL sets an uncertainty-guided pseudo-labeling (UGPL) strategy to generate high-confidence pseudo-labels for incremental training. The framework supports end-to-end training with multiple concurrent objectives. Extensive experiments on two fine-grained datasets validate the effectiveness of the proposed model. FineCL outperforms the state-of-the-art method by maximum of 6.6% improvement in long-step continual panoptic segmentation tasks.
author: Bo Yuan, Danpei Zhao, Tian Li, Wentao Li, Bowen Yang, Zhenwei Shi

title: Mitigating Temporal Domain Disparities for Cross-Temporal Remote Sensing Semantic Change Detection Using Vector Parcel Data and Contrastive Learning
content: Most existing change detection (CD) methods assume that multitemporal remote sensing images are acquired under similar seasonal conditions. When this assumption is violated, significant phenological variations introduce large appearance discrepancies that severely degrade detection accuracy. Moreover, most deep-learning-based CD approaches rely solely on raster imagery and neglect historical vector parcel data, resulting in limited training samples and poor generalization in real-world applications. To address these challenges, this article proposes a vector-guided cross-temporal semantic CD (SCD) framework that mitigates temporal appearance discrepancies through contrastive feature alignment. A boundary-preserved superpixel segmentation and vector parcel-constrained adaptive cropping strategy is employed to automatically generate vector-aligned training samples, followed by a category-constrained clustering scheme to suppress label noise. A hybrid cross-temporal learning framework is then designed to reduce feature-level discrepancies between temporally mismatched images using Fourier-enhanced representations and contrastive supervision. Semantic changes are finally identified at the vector parcel level through rule-based reasoning. Experiments on two real-world regional datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves robust parcel-level SCD under significant seasonal variations, outperforming representative state-of-the-art approaches and exhibiting strong generalization capability.
author: Shengli Wang, Nanshan Zheng, Yihu Zhu, Wei Liu, Ming Hao, Erzhu Li, Yilin Kuang, Yi Jiang, Lianpeng Zhang

title: Toward Reliable Disparity Estimation: Uncertainty-Aware Stereo Matching Framework for Satellite Images
content: As a critical task in the field of Earth observation, satellite images stereo matching (SISM) aims to estimate disparity from a pair of epipolar rectified images and reconstruct the 3-D structure of the Earth’s surface. However, the local features in repetitive patterns and texture-less regions of satellite images (collectively referred to as ill-posed regions) are highly similar, causing existing SISM methods to produce unreliable disparity estimations. To address this issue, this article proposes a novel uncertainty-aware SISM framework (UA-SISM). First, a disparity estimation network is employed to construct a disparity space containing multiple coarse disparity samples. To quantitatively analyze the reliability of the model’s predictions, we model the disparity as Gaussian distribution and design a distribution-level uncertainty estimator (DLUE). The DLUE computes distribution-level uncertainty by comparing differences among multiple disparity samples. Based on this, we derive a specific loss function for estimating distribution-level uncertainty. Next, we adopt an uncertainty-error alignment (UEA) strategy to promote the coconvergence of disparity error and distribution-level uncertainty, thereby improving the reliability of the model’s predictions. Finally, we compute sample-level uncertainty to refine the disparity in the disparity space. This further enhances model robustness in ill-posed regions. Extensive experiments on two publicly available SISM datasets show that UA-SISM achieves state-of-the-art performance and obtains optimal results in ill-posed regions of satellite images. Our code is available at: https://github.com/Henryrjh/UA-SISM
author: Jiahao Rao, Rui Liu, Jun Chen, Xin Tian

title: Implicit Optical Flow-Guided Network for Infrared Small-Target Tracking
content: Infrared small-target tracking (ISTT) holds significant practical value in both military and civilian applications. Currently, some studies achieve ISTT by using optical flow-based methods. However, existing optical flow methods typically rely on pixel-level motion estimation or use flow maps as auxiliary network input and struggle to apply flow fields directly for bounding box localization, which further degrades tracking stability. Furthermore, in low signal-to-noise ratio infrared sequences, explicit optical flow frequently exhibits high errors and costs. To address these challenges, we propose a complementary and optical flow-like fusion network (COFNet), which constructs a flow-like field at high-level feature maps to produce temporally enhanced feature maps that improve cross-frame consistency of bounding boxes and enable robust association. Specifically, we propose an optical flow-like feature fusion (OFF) module that computes interframe similarity and displacement relationships across multiscale feature maps and temporally corrects current features while integrating cross-scale information, thereby making the generated bounding boxes easier to associate. This module establishes an implicit flow field at the feature level to utilize interframe motion information without the computational cost of explicit optical flow, thereby improving robustness to occlusion and localization stability during tracking. To compensate for lost positional cues and reduce mismatches caused by box displacement during tracking, we design a channel splitting and complementary mapping (CSM) module that divides feature channels into semantic and spatial subspaces and fuses them via recalibration. To suppress false positives and missed detections that disrupt trajectory continuity, a block-wise self-attention (BSA) module that enhances responses to fine textures and local context around the target. Finally, we employ DeepOC-SORT at the detection output to identity assignment. Experimental results on public datasets demonstrate that the proposed method attains superior tracking performance.
author: Bin Xiao, Xingyu Zhou, Yue Hu

title: Compress–Align–Detect: Onboard Change Detection From Unregistered Images
content: Change detection (CD) from satellite images typically incurs a delay ranging from several hours up to days because of latency in downlinking the acquired images and generating orthorectified image products at the ground stations; this may preclude real or near real-time applications. To overcome this limitation, we propose shifting the entire CD workflow onboard satellites. This requires simultaneously solving challenges in data storage, image registration, and CD with a strict complexity constraint. In this article, we present a novel and efficient framework for onboard CD that addresses the aforementioned challenges in an end-to-end fashion with a deep neural network composed of three interlinked submodules: 1) image compression, tailored to minimize onboard data storage resources; 2) lightweight coregistration of nonorthorectified multitemporal image pairs; and 3) a novel temporally invariant and computationally efficient CD model. This is the first approach in the literature combining all these tasks in a single end-to-end framework under the constraints dictated by onboard processing. Experimental results compare each submodule with the current state-of-the-art and evaluate the performance of the overall integrated system in a realistic setting on low-power hardware. Compelling CD results are obtained in terms of  $F1$ -score as a function of compression rate, sustaining a throughput of 0.7 Mpixel/s on a 15-W accelerator.
author: Gabriele Inzerillo, Diego Valsesia, Aniello Fiengo, Enrico Magli

title: Pointwise Frequency Attention and Coarse-to-Fine Learning With Image-to-Instance Level Supervision for Fine-Grained Object Detection in Remote Sensing Images
content: Fine-grained object detection (FGOD) models aim to distinguish different subcategories within the same coarse-grained category. Although two-stage object detection frameworks are extensively employed in current FGOD models due to their high detection accuracy, they still face two major problems. First, insufficient discriminability of the backbone features results in frequent misclassification among similar subcategory objects. Second, the limited capability of the backbone features to represent object direction leads to localization deviation. To address the first problem, a coarse-to-fine image-level supervised feature discriminability enhancement (C2FIm-FDE) module is proposed. Guided by the generated coarse- and fine-grained image-level category labels, the C2FIm-FDE module progressively strengthens the discriminability of backbone features at the image level. To tackle the second problem, a pointwise frequency attention based on the discrete wavelet transform (PFA-DWT) is proposed. By adaptively combining multiband frequency features and leveraging the dual localization capability of the DWT in the spatial and frequency domains, the PFA-DWT module achieves accurate fusion of frequency-domain features encoding local object orientation information with spatial features via a pointwise attention mechanism. To further enhance the discriminability of similar subcategories, a coarse-to-fine instance-level supervised detection (C2FIn) head is proposed, which gradually focuses on differences among subcategories within the same coarse-grained category by sequentially introducing coarse- and fine-grained instance-level labels. Ablation studies validate the effectiveness of the C2FIm-FDE, PFA-DWT modules, and C2FIn head, as well as their arbitrary combinations. Quantitative comparisons with popular FGOD models on the two large-scale public datasets, FAIR1M-1.0 and FAIR1M-2.0, demonstrate the excellent detection accuracy and generalization capability of our FGOD model. Source code is available at https://github.com/PFA-DWT/PFA-C2FI
author: Xiaoliang Qian, Xiaobin Li, Wei Wang, Xiwen Yao, Gong Cheng

title: CDMask: Change Customized Mask Architecture for Change Detection
