content: Remote sensing change detection (RSCD) aims to compare two or more images recorded for the same area but taken at different time stamps to quantitatively and qualitatively assess changes in geographical entities and environmental factors. Mainstream models usually built on pixel-by-pixel change detection paradigms, which cannot tolerate the diversity of changes due to complex scenes and variation in imaging conditions. Recently, some approaches have attempted to perform RSCD directly based on mask classification. However, they are inefficient and perform suboptimally. In this article, we provide an in-depth analysis of the change characteristic of RSCD and further propose the corresponding: 1) meta-architecture CDMask and 2) an enhanced instance network CDMaskFormer. Components of CDMask include Siamese backbone, change extractor, pixel decoder, Transformer decoder, and change customized normalized detector, which accommodates the change characteristics of the RSCD and ensures the proper functioning of the mask detection paradigm. Since the change query can be adaptively updated based on the bi-temporal feature content, the proposed CDMask can adapt to different latent data distributions, thus accurately identifying regions of interest changes in complex scenarios. Then, we further propose an enhanced instance network CDMaskFormer for the change detection task, which includes: 1) a spatial–temporal convolutional attention-based instantiated change extractor to capture spatio-temporal context simultaneously with lightweight operations and 2) a scene-guided axial attention-instantiated Transformer decoder to extract more spatial details. State-of-the-art performance of CDMaskFormer is achieved on five benchmark datasets with a satisfactory efficiency–accuracy tradeoff. In particular, we construct a GAZACD dataset to explore the performance of CDMaskFormer in the real world. By analyzing 4353 pairs of images of the Gaza surface during and after the conflict, CDMaskFormer is able to effectively reveal the surface changes that occurred in the Gaza Strip and guide the post-disaster reconstruction. The code and dataset are available at https://github.com/xwmaxwma/rschange
author: Xiaowen Ma, Zhenkai Wu, Jiatong Pan, Kai Zheng, Rongrong Lian, Wangyu Wu, Zhenhua Huang, Yun Chen, Renxiang Guan, Rong Fu, Simon Fong, Wei Zhang

title: HyperR3SNet: Leveraging Hyperbolic Space and Vision Foundation Models for Remote Sensing Semantic Segmentation
content: Remote sensing semantic segmentation is driven by land-use monitoring, urban planning, and ecological assessment, yet progress is hampered by scarce pixel-level labels. To address this issue, we present HyperR3SNet, which is an efficient framework for remote sensing semantic segmentation that tackles data scarcity and scale variations in overhead imagery. HyperR3SNet transfers self-supervised vision foundation models (VFMs) to remote sensing, providing strong feature generalization with minimal labeled data. Building on this, a multiscale cross-attention (MSCA) module is inserted into the backbone, Vision Transformer layer, enabling the network to extract richer features across widely varying object scales. To keep the model lightweight, a matrix-factorized task head is employed, sharply reducing parameters and computation while sustaining accuracy. HyperR3SNet is the first model to integrate a hyperbolic pixel-level loss with VFM adaptation for cross-domain and low-annotation remote sensing segmentation, leveraging the exponential expansion property of hyperbolic geometry to capture latent interclass relations and preserve structural consistency under weak supervision. Evaluated on the widely adopted remote sensing segmentation datasets (e.g., iSAID, LoveDA, Potsdam, and Vaihingen), HyperR3SNet achieves mean intersection over union (mIoU) of 67.60%, 55.86%, 80.07%, and 96.46%, respectively, and on average surpasses a broad range of state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods.
author: Junjie Fu, Chenliang Wang, Mingzhe Liu, Xinghua Li, Yi Liu, Wenjiao Shi, Ruili Wang

title: Unsupervised Snowy-Weather Point Cloud Denoising via Two-Stage Filter-Network Collaboration
content: Reliable light detection and ranging (LiDAR) point cloud data is fundamental to real-time outdoor perception applications such as autonomous driving and mobile robotics; however, snow-induced noise severely disrupts point cloud structure and significantly degrades the performance of downstream algorithms. Although denoising can mitigate such effects, existing approaches face notable limitations: filter-based methods require no labels but suffer from slow inference, making them difficult to deploy in real-time, whereas supervised learning methods offer fast inference but rely on large-scale annotated data that are scarce in adverse weather, which hampers generalization. To address these challenges, we propose a two-stage unsupervised snow-noise removal framework that achieves both high accuracy and real-time performance. In the first stage, a dynamic multiparameter radius outlier removal (DMROR) filter leverages the low-intensity and near-range characteristics of snow noise to estimate snowfall intensity and adaptively adjust neighborhood radius and density thresholds, enabling precise noise identification and pseudo-label generation. In the second stage, a lightweight denoising network, RPSnowNet, is trained on pseudo-labels generated by DMROR, avoiding the need for manual annotations. RPSnowNet adopts a dual-branch range–point architecture enhanced by the proposed range–point fusion module (RPFM) and multilevel fusion head module (MFHM) for efficient feature interaction, while the postprocess module further refines predictions using snow-noise priors. Experiments on the WADS and CADC datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) unsupervised snow removal performance with a real-time inference latency of 15 ms. The denoised, high-quality point cloud data significantly improves the performance of downstream sensing tasks, thereby enhancing the reliability of the sensing system under snow conditions.
author: Wenfeng Leng, Yonghui Huang, Xi Zhang, Chuan Hu

title: GNSS-IR Scattering Model Considering Sea Surface Wind Direction and Its Application
content: In recent years, the rapid development of the global navigation satellite system (GNSS) has led to the emergence of a new ground-based remote sensing technique, namely GNSS interferometric reflectometry (GNSS-IR). In marine applications, GNSS-IR research has mainly focused on tide-level retrieval, and several recent studies have also demonstrated its potential for significant wave height estimation. This study aims to further investigate whether GNSS-IR can be used for sea-surface wind-direction estimation. To address this question, it is first necessary to start from the physical mechanism and construct scattering-model simulations of wind-direction-sensitive signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) feature parameters under different wave-direction conditions. However, mechanistic studies on wind-direction modeling for ground-based GNSS-IR remain absent. Building on the original Z–V scattering model, this study introduces a two-scale sea-surface scattering framework and adjusts the antenna gain setting to match the conventional receiver mode. By simultaneously considering the geometric modulation effect of wind direction on large-scale wave undulations and the local electromagnetic scattering contribution of small-scale roughness structures, a sea-surface scattering mechanism model capable of reflecting the modulation effect of wind-direction changes on scattered power is established. Based on this scattering model, the variation patterns of left-hand circularly polarized (LHCP) and right-hand circularly polarized (RHCP) signal power under different wind-direction conditions are analyzed. On this basis, an SNR-based parameter capable of identifying wind-direction characteristics is proposed, namely the ratio of scattered energy between low and high elevation angles. Experimental data collected at station CALC in Louisiana, USA, during Hurricane delta were used for validation. The results show that GNSS-IR wind-direction estimation yields two possible orientations, meaning that it cannot distinguish between the incoming and outgoing wind directions. For the first time, this study establishes a wind-direction-identifiable feature parameter based on the physical scattering mechanism, and experimentally demonstrates that this parameter exhibits a certain sensitivity to wind direction, which is of great significance for advancing GNSS-IR wind-direction estimation and improving its underlying physical mechanism.
author: Xiaolei Wang, Yang Nan, Xiaotong Yang, Ting Liao, Xiufeng He

title: Synergies Between GNSS-R and Radiometer Stokes Parameters Seen From SMAP
content: This work presents the first time- and space-collocated comparison between Stokes parameters from radiometric emission and L2 global navigation satellite system (GNSS)-R forward scattering, using data from the soil moisture active passive (SMAP) radiometer and SMAP-reflectometer. Radiometer ( $\boldsymbol {I},\boldsymbol {Q}$ ,  $\boldsymbol {U}$ , and  $\boldsymbol {V}$ ) and reflectometer ( $\boldsymbol {S}_{0}$ ,  $\boldsymbol {S}_{1}$ ,  $\boldsymbol {S}_{2}$ , and  $\boldsymbol {S}_{3}$ ) parameters, along with the degree of polarization (DoP), are analyzed across International Geosphere–Biosphere Programme (IGBP) land cover types. Pearson correlation revealed strong positive relationships between linear polarization parameters ( $\boldsymbol {Q}$ ,  $\boldsymbol {S}_{1}$ , and  $\boldsymbol {S}_{2}$ ) over bare soils and croplands, and negative correlations over water and snow, reflecting differences in emission and reflection mechanisms. Canonical correlation analysis (CCA) provides a multivariate view of structural alignment, identifying high canonical correlations ( $\ge$ 0.73) in closed shrublands, open shrublands, and snow/ice, and much lower values in water and urban classes. Linear discriminant analysis (LDA) assesses biome-level separability using fused GNSS-R and radiometer features, achieving near-perfect classification for closed shrublands, urban, and wetlands while confirming low separability for classes, such as water, grasslands, and open shrublands, that also show weak CCA alignment. A taxonomy integrates these metrics groups land cover classes into optimal retrieval strategy categories, enabling biome-tailored data fusion approaches. Their fusion may enable improved geophysical retrievals in land surface characterization, provided that hybrid models account for land cover-specific scattering mechanisms and the varying degree of sensor alignment.
author: Nereida Rodriguez-Alvarez, Xavier Bosch-Lluis, Kamal Oudrhiri

title: Deconvolutive Optimized General Linear Chirplet Transform and Its Application in Hydrocarbon Detection
content: Time–frequency analysis (TFA) methods are widely used techniques for local frequency analysis and seismic attribute extraction, playing a significant role in hydrocarbon-bearing reservoir prediction and geological structure interpretation. The general linear Chirplet transform (GLCT) has been widely adopted in TFA due to its superior time resolution. However, the GLCT suffers from the limitation of a fixed time window width, which restricts its adaptability to signals with varying frequency components. Furthermore, GLCT can only maintain optimal time–frequency resolution within localized frequency bands and is prone to introducing artifacts in low-frequency regions. To further enhance the time–frequency resolution, we propose a new TFA method named deconvolutive optimized GLCT (DOGLCT). The methodology consists of two main steps: first, a four-parameter-controlled Gaussian window function is introduced as the time window for optimized GLCT (OGLCT). The width of this four-parameter Gaussian window adaptively adjusts in response to frequency variations, thus ensuring guaranteed time–frequency resolution across arbitrary frequency bands. Subsequently, the time–frequency spectrum (TFS) obtained from OGLCT undergoes deconvolution with the point spread function (PSF), ultimately yielding the refined TFS of DOGLCT. The TFA results of synthetic seismic signals demonstrate that DOGLCT outperforms the conventional GLCT in terms of TF resolution and effectively addresses the issue of low-frequency artifacts. Tests on noisy seismic data validate the superior noise robustness of DOGLCT. Finally, the application of DOGLCT to field data for hydrocarbon detection confirms the enhanced time resolution of the proposed method, enabling precise identification and delineation of hydrocarbon-bearing reservoir distributions.
author: Huixing Zhang, Wangyang Wang, Bingshou He, Yancang Feng

title: High-Resolution Core MRI 3-D Imaging Method Based on Fusion of Image Linear Interpolation and DCGAN
content: As oil and gas reservoir development advances, core nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) imaging plays a vital role in permeability analysis and fluid distribution visualization. However, conventional NMR imaging can only provide 2-D slice information, making it difficult to fully characterize the 3-D structural features of cores. The 3-D reconstruction based on simple stacking methods often yields unsatisfactory results, posing a challenge for accurate core NMR 3-D imaging. To overcome these limitations, this study proposes a novel core NMR 3-D imaging approach that integrates image linear interpolation with a deep convolutional generative adversarial network (DCGAN). Proton density-weighted NMR imaging is used to obtain 2-D cross-sectional images of the core. These images are processed through generative adversarial training, combining image interpolation and DCGAN to generate transitional images between adjacent slices. Based on the generated transitional images, 3-D reconstruction of the core is achieved, and 3-D visualization of the fluid distribution is obtained via threshold segmentation. The accuracy of the proposed method is verified using synthetic rock models. Furthermore, water displacement experiments were conducted on cores with different permeability levels (ultralow, low, medium, and high). Oil-saturated cores were displaced by heavy water, and the resulting 3-D fluid distribution images were reconstructed using the proposed approach. Experimental results demonstrate that this method can effectively reveal the spatial characteristics of fluid distribution under varying permeability and displacement conditions, providing a new visualization tool for permeability evaluation and fluid mobility analysis in oil and gas reservoirs.
author: Bo Li, Maojin Tan, Siyu Wang, Changyu Wang

title: Physics-Informed Guided Reconstruction: Electromagnetic Data Anomalous Noise Suppression via Spatial Mask Awareness
content: Electromagnetic (EM) surveying serves as a vital geophysical exploration method, widely applied in resource detection, environmental monitoring, and related fields. However, the acquisition of EM data is often contaminated by irregularly distributed and intensity-varying anomalous noise, which severely degrades data quality and affects subsequent interpretation accuracy. To address this challenge, this article innovatively proposes a physics-informed guided reconstruction framework for EM data. In the first stage, an anomalous recognition network integrating cyclic cross-learning and energy distribution constraint is designed to achieve precise localization of anomalous noise. In the second stage, a diffusion-based recovery model conditioned on the physical attenuation law and the region mask is constructed, which realizes focused reconstruction of anomalous regions and obeys attenuation frequency distribution through mask-oriented modulation. Experiments both in synthetic and real data demonstrate that the proposed method achieves optimal quantitative metrics under various signal-to-noise ratios (SNRs) and data missing rates, while effectively separating noise and preserving the 3-D spatial continuity of the EM data. This work provides reliable theoretical and experimental support for high-precision EM data reconstruction.
author: Cao Song, Chenliang Liu, Yalin Wang, Wenkai Lu, Zhuo Jia, Shugang Ye, Weihua Yao, Xianxu Zhang

title: Hybrid F-K Filtering and Deep Learning for P/S Separation in DAS VSP Data
content: The accurate separation of P-waves and P–S converted waves in distributed acoustic sensing (DAS) vertical seismic profiling (VSP) data is crucial for underground imaging. Traditional methods suffer from severe artifacts, and standard polarization analysis is inapplicable to single-fiber measurements. While pure end-to-end deep learning methods are powerful, their application to actual field data is often bottlenecked by the scarcity of real-world labels. In this study, we propose FKPSN, representing a novel physics-guided task-simplification paradigm. By leveraging F-K filtering as a macroscopic kinematic prior, we fundamentally transform the end-to-end separation into a highly tractable, physics-constrained error-correction task. To explicitly address the mutual energy leakage introduced by this initial F-K separation, we tailor a dual-branch convolutional neural network (CNN) equipped with a feature fusion module. This design enforces interactive cross-branch communication to simulate cross-component interference cancellation, dynamically reallocating leaked wave energy to the correct components. Extensive experiments on synthetic and field DAS VSP data demonstrate that FKPSN achieves high-fidelity separation in both time and frequency domains. Crucially, comprehensive velocity generalization tests reveal that the network possesses a robust active kinematic error-correction capability, successfully recovering true wavefields even when the initial F-K velocity parameters contain severe deviations.
author: Jilei Sui, Zhicheng Zhong, Yue Li, Qiankun Feng

title: Numerical Study on Anisotropic Permeability Inversion From Dipole Seismoelectric Logging in Fluid-Saturated Porous Formations
content: In situ determination of permeability anisotropy is important for unconventional reservoir productivity, such as in the evaluation of hydraulic fracturing effectiveness. We investigate a method of permeability–anisotropy inversion using dipole seismoelectric logging. A 3-D numerical algorithm combining finite-difference and -element methods in cylindrical coordinates is proposed to simulate dipole seismoelectric logging in fluid-saturated porous formations with transversely isotropic (TI) permeability. Calculation results reveal that the flexural-wave-induced electric field (Ef wave) exhibits high sensitivity to permeability variations, and its amplitude and phase delay increase significantly as the porosity and permeability decrease. Wave attenuation from the  $\theta _{0} = 0^{\circ }$  dipole source effectively inverts horizontal permeability ( $\kappa _{H}$ ). Crucially, wave attenuation from the  $\theta _{0} = 90^{\circ }$  source reliably inverts vertical permeability ( $\kappa _{V}$ ) unless the borehole is nearly parallel to the symmetry axis. Using the radial electric field component of the Ef wave provides a significantly higher inversion accuracy for both  $\kappa _{H}$  and  $\kappa _{V}$ , especially in low-porosity and -permeability formations, compared with those using the flexural wave. As the difference between the horizontal and vertical permeabilities increases, the relative inversion errors increase significantly. The greater the difference between  $\kappa _{H}$  and  $\kappa _{V}$ , the larger could be their inversion errors. Furthermore, a new relationship is identified where the amplitude ratio  $E_{z}(\theta _{0})$ / $E_{\mathrm {z}}$ (90°) approximates either  $\kappa _{rr}$ (90°)/ $\kappa _{rr}$ ( $\theta _{0}$ ) (when  $\kappa _{H} \gt \kappa _{V}$ ) or  $\kappa _{rr}$ ( $\theta _{0}$ )/ $\kappa _{rr}$ (90°) (when  $\kappa _{H} \lt \kappa _{V}$ ). Accordingly, an equation for estimating the anisotropic permeability orientation is derived, achieving relative errors of less than 12%. Even at high noise levels, it can maintain good robustness. We conclusively demonstrate the significant advantages of dipole seismoelectric logging for accurately inverting anisotropic formation permeability.
author: Xing Kou, Wei Guan, Chao Zhang, Xiao He, Jun Wang, Hengshan Hu

title: A Multisource Time-Series Remote Sensing Data Fusion Network for High-Spatial-Resolution Crop Classification
content: High-spatial-resolution multisource remote sensing image fusion enables precise delineation of crop boundaries and spatial patterns, and distinguishing fine-grained categories that are difficult to separate at medium resolution. This provides a refined and reliable technique for classifying small and fragmented crops with imbalanced category distributions. However, images acquired from different sensors vary in both resolution and semantic characteristics. Effectively mitigating resolution mismatches and leveraging complementary information remains an open challenge. To address this challenge, we propose a multisource and multiresolution image fusion framework. The framework is designed to synergistically integrate remote sensing data of different resolutions, thereby enhancing crop classification accuracy. First, we introduce the multireceptive-field residual block (MRRB). It incorporates multiple receptive field pathways through multiscale dilated-convolution branches, thereby strengthening the model's ability to capture multiscale texture and boundary features of crops. By preserving spatial resolution and enriching contextual information via residual connections, MRRB effectively characterizes crop spatial structure and distribution. Its compact design and moderate parameter volume significantly improve classification accuracy. In addition, we design a multisource multitemporal (MSMT) fusion module to jointly model dynamic temporal variations and spectral differences across multisource time series. This module adaptively balances the contributions of different data sources through parallel temporal and spectral attention branches combined with learnable fusion weights, thereby enhancing the integrated representation of spatiotemporal, spectral, and polarization features. Extensive experiments demonstrate that, while maintaining computational efficiency, the proposed MRRB and MSMT modules together outperform existing state-of-the-art methods in high-resolution crop classification tasks.
author: Beibei Yu, Xin Huang, Hengmao Xiang, Jie Yang, Lilin Tu, Yujuan Zhang, Yinghua Jiao

title: A Cross-Hierarchical Difference Feature Fusion Network Based on Multiscale Encoder–Decoder for Coastal Wetlands Hyperspectral Change Detection
content: Hyperspectral change detection (HCD) is one of the core applications of remote sensing imagery, and it holds profound significance and research value for monitoring the dynamic changes of coastal wetlands with extremely high ecological value. However, existing methods often fail to fully capture the multiscale spatial–spectral features of coastal wetland changes, and they inadequately fuse differential feature information. To address these issues, this article proposes a cross-hierarchical difference feature fusion network (CHDFFN) based on a multiscale encoder–decoder. Taking a customized encoder–decoder as its backbone, this network integrates a multiscale feature extraction subnetwork with residual connections and a dual-kernel channel-spatial attention module, enabling the multilevel extraction and initial fusion of spatial–spectral features of coastal wetlands. The encoder uses convolutional blocks with varying receptive fields to capture multiscale representations ranging from shallow details to deep semantics; while the decoder fuses output results via skip connections to restore spatial resolution and suppress noise. In addition, the spatial–spectral change feature learning module is used to learn hierarchical change representations, and the adaptive high-level feature fusion module dynamically balances the contributions of hierarchical differential features through adaptive weight assignment—enhancing the model’s ability to characterize the complex changes of coastal wetlands. Finally, experimental results on four public datasets show that: compared with state-of-the-art methods, this model achieves an average maximum improvement of 4.61% in overall accuracy, 19.79% in the Kappa coefficient, and 18.90% in the F1-score, verifying its effectiveness in coastal wetland change detection. In the field of remote sensing, accurate and reliable spectral–spatial change detection is crucial for coastal wetland monitoring and environmental management.
author: Mingshuai Sheng, Uzair Aslam Bhatti, Junfeng Zhang, Siling Feng, Yonis Gulzar, Muhammad Attique Khan, Dilmurod Turaev, Anorgul Ashirova

title: Spatial-Offset Guided and Contrastive Feature Rectification Network for Improved Land Use Classification With Optical and SAR Imagery
content: Fusing synthetic aperture radar (SAR) and optical imagery provides more comprehensive information for land use classification. However, significant modality disparities often lead to residual misalignment between the co-registered images, even after conventional geometric correction. In addition, unimodal feature extraction can be negatively affected by sensor noise and cross-modal inconsistencies, potentially yielding unreliable representations. To address these challenges, we propose a novel network architecture, which integrates two fusion strategies. First, the spatial-offset guided alignment module aims to addresses spatial discrepancies between two modalities, which adaptively align feature deviations in SAR data. Second, the unimodality-rectified contrastive fusion module employs supervised signals from unimodal segmentation branches to preserve reliable, task-relevant semantic information. Moreover, to capture both global contexts and local details, the network incorporates an advanced Mamba-based encoder. Extensive experiments on three datasets demonstrates the superior performance of our approach: On the WHU-OPT-SAR dataset, it outperforms the suboptimal method by 0.7 percentage points in overall accuracy and 1.0 percentage points in the Kappa coefficient; on the DDHR dataset, the improvements are 0.6 and 0.9 percentage points, respectively; and on the DFC2020 dataset, the corresponding increments reach 1.3 and 1.6 percentage points, respectively. Additional spatial offsets analysis also illustrates the robustness and generality of our approach.
author: Kai Xu, Anling Wang, Shuyue Zhang, Ranyu Shi

title: Addressing Severe Class Imbalance in Spatio-Temporal Landslide Detection With Focal Tversky Loss
content: Applying artificial intelligence (AI) to landslide detection is often limited by poor data quality in remote sensing, specifically extreme class imbalance and signal noise. In satellite image time series, landslides cover only a tiny fraction of the area, creating a weak signal that standard models struggle to learn. This study uses the large-scale Sen12Landslides dataset to test if specialized loss functions can solve these data quality problems without needing extra data sources like digital elevation model or Synthetic Aperture Radar. We compared standard loss functions against linear and focal Tversky losses (FTLs) within the U-Net with temporal attention encoder framework. Our results show that the FTL, which focuses on difficult examples, significantly reduces the impact of class imbalance. The method achieved an intersection over union of 0.468 and an F1-score of 0.638 on Sentinel-2 images, beating the baseline by effectively ignoring background noise and detecting small, fragmented landslides. These findings show that improving the loss function is a highly efficient way to handle data quality limitations in AI-based hazard monitoring.
author: Vytaras Juraska, Wei-Che Chien

title: LiDAR, GNSS, and IMU Sensor Fine Alignment Through Dynamic Time Warping to Construct 3-D City Maps
content: Light detection and ranging (LiDAR)-based 3-D mapping suffers from cumulative drift causing global misalignment, particularly in global navigation satellite system (GNSS)-constrained environments. To address this, we propose a unified framework that fuses LiDAR, GNSS, and inertial measurement unit (IMU) data for high-resolution city-scale mapping. The method performs velocity-based temporal alignment using dynamic time warping and refines GNSS and IMU signals via extended Kalman filtering. Local maps are built using normal distributions transform-based registration and pose graph optimization with loop closure detection, while global consistency is enforced using GNSS-constrained anchors followed by fine registration of overlapping segments. We also introduce a large-scale multimodal dataset captured in Perth, Western Australia to facilitate future research in this direction. Our dataset comprises 144 000 frames acquired with a 128-channel Ouster LiDAR, synchronized real-time kinematic (RTK)–GNSS trajectories, and microelectromechanical systems (MEMS)–IMU measurements across 21 urban loops. To assess geometric consistency, we evaluated our method using alignment metrics based on road centerlines and intersections to capture both global and local accuracy. The proposed framework reduces the average global alignment error from 3.32 to 1.24 m, achieving a 61.4% improvement, and significantly decreases the intersection centroid offset from 13.22 to 2.01 m, corresponding to an 84.8% enhancement. The constructed high-fidelity map and raw dataset are publicly available through IEEE Dataport and its visualization can be viewed in the provided Demo. This dataset and method together establish a new benchmark for evaluating 3-D city mapping in GNSS-constrained environments, with source code available at GitHub Repository.
author: Haitian Wang, Hezam Albaqami, Xinyu Wang, Muhammad Ibrahim, Zainy M. Malakan, Abdullah M. Algamdi, Mohammed H. Alghamdi, Ajmal Mian

title: Topside Ionospheric Characterization and NeQuick2 Model Evaluation Based on Tianmu-1 and COSMIC-2 Observations
content: The topside ionosphere, extending from the F2-layer peak toward the plasmasphere, plays an essential role in satellite navigation and space weather applications. However, its spatiotemporal variability remains poorly characterized due to the lack of global high-resolution observations. In this study, GNSS precise orbit determination data from the Tianmu-1 and COSMIC-2 low-Earth orbit constellations are employed to construct global topside ionospheric maps during equinox and solstice periods in 2024, corresponding to the high solar activity phase of Solar Cycle 25. The spatiotemporal characteristics of topside total electron content (TTEC) above 520 km are analyzed, and the performance of the NeQuick2 model is systematically evaluated. Results show that LEO-derived TTEC exhibits pronounced diurnal and seasonal variations, with daytime maxima occurring between 12:00 and 16:00 local time. The nighttime contribution of topside TTEC reaches 60.62% of the total TEC, significantly exceeding the daytime contribution of 43.18%, indicating an enhanced role of the topside ionosphere at night. Spatially, LEO-derived TTEC does not present a distinct equatorial ionization anomaly double-peak structure, whereas NeQuick2 reproduces this feature while exhibiting a systematic underestimation of approximately 5.46 TECu. Moreover, NeQuick2 shows limited sensitivity to seasonal variability and reduced diurnal variation in the topside TEC ratio, with regional overestimation in parts of the winter hemisphere. Sensitivity analyses further reveal a strong dependence of NeQuick2 performance on the treatment of the solar radio flux F10.7. Under high solar activity, the constrained scheme provides more stable results in the winter hemisphere, while removing the F10.7 constraint improves the representation of enhanced topside plasma densities in the summer hemisphere. These findings provide observational constraints for topside ionospheric modeling under high solar activity and offer guidance for improving the NeQuick2 model.
author: Haojie Liu, Xingliang Huo, Ting Zhang, Yunbin Yuan, Kaixin Wang, Weihong Sun, Qi Liu, Min Li

title: Multisource Data Integration Study on the Spatiotemporal Characteristics and Driving Mechanisms of Ground Displacement: Huainan City, China, Case Study
content: Huainan City is a major energy base in China, characterized by complex geological conditions and frequent ground deformation driven by multiple factors. Previous studies have often focused on single causes, making it difficult to fully reveal the combined drivers of ground deformation. There is, therefore, an urgent need to integrate multisource, multimodal data for comprehensive analysis. In this study, Small Baseline Subset Interferometry Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR) processing of 150 Sentinel-1A scenes was used to obtain ground deformation information in Huainan from January 2019 to December 2023. These deformation data were combined with Gravity Recovery and Climate Experiment Follow-On (GRACE-FO) groundwater measurements, population density, road density, and gross domestic product to perform factor detection and interaction factor detection using the optimal parameter-based geographical detector (OPGD) method, quantitatively assessing the relationships between deformation and multiple influencing factors. The results show significant regional variability in deformation rates across Huainan, with maximum deformation and uplift rates of 127 mm/year and 63 mm/year occurring in active and closed mines, respectively. Groundwater change plays a key role in ground deformation, exhibiting a hydrology-dominated, multifactor coupling pattern. Cross-validation with leveling and well measurements confirms the high reliability of both the InSAR-derived deformation and GRACE-FO inverted groundwater storage changes; the trends in groundwater storage and ground deformation both display periodic fluctuations. Finally, detailed analyses of three characteristic zones, namely the urban district, the mining area, and a ground-mounted photovoltaic power plant. This study developed a comprehensive analysis framework for ground deformation based on multisource, multimodal data fusion and the OPGD method, and systematically quantified the coupled relationships between deformation and multiple driving factors in Huainan. The framework demonstrates good scalability and can provide scientific support for long-term monitoring and risk management in resource-based cities with similar geological conditions.
author: Zisu Cheng, Qingbiao Guo, Meinan Zheng, Lei Wang, Songbo Wu, Sen Du, José Fernández

title: StyleDe-CD: Dual-Level Style Disentanglement for Semisupervised Change Detection
content: Semisupervised change detection (SSCD) is a promising approach for reducing the annotation burden in bitemporal remote sensing imagery. However, learning robust representations remains challenging due to style variations across and within temporal images, which often dominate visual differences and interfere with semantic change modeling. These challenges can be attributed to two levels of style-semantic entanglement: 1) cross-temporal style discrepancies caused by acquisition variations (e.g., illumination shifts and seasonal changes), which may confuse the model and increase sensitivity to superficial appearance differences; and 2) intratemporal style heterogeneity introduced by diverse background characteristics (e.g., land-cover diversity and vegetation density differences), which can bias feature learning and obscure meaningful semantic changes. To address these challenges, we propose StyleDe-CD, a dual-level style disentanglement framework for SSCD. At the input level, we design a stochastic spectralphotometric style augmentation (SSPSA) strategy, which combines spectral style alignment and photometric transformations to mitigate cross-temporal discrepancies and enhance robustness to appearance variations. At the feature level, we introduce an intratemporal style mixing (ISM) mechanism that randomly mixes feature statistics across samples within a minibatch, encouraging the model to suppress background-specific styles and focus on semantic changes. Extensive experiments on the LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, and CDD benchmarks demonstrate that StyleDe-CD consistently achieves strong and competitive performance compared to existing semisupervised baselines, particularly under lowlabel regimes, indicating its effectiveness in SSCD.
author: Ze Zhang, Yue Zhou, Linqing Huang, Zhentao Zou, Xue Jiang, Jianxin Xing, Xingzhao Liu

title: Adaptive Rotation-Scaling Ensemble Patch Attack for Ship Detection in Remote Sensing Images
content: Existing adversarial patch generation methods for remote sensing ship detection suffer from several limitations, including insufficient adaptability to patch size, inability to handle orientation variations of rotated ships, and poor transferability of adversarial patches. Considering these problems, this article first proposes an adaptive rotation-scaling ensemble patch attack method, named ARSEA, to enhance suitability of adversarial patches for ship sizes and orientations. Specifically, a adversarial patch is scaled according to the size of the rotated bounding box of the ship and rotated using the angle information combined with a location-aware mask, enabling precise alignment with ship targets of various sizes and orientations. For the last problem, a model ensemble attack strategy based on gradient direction consistency is proposed, where weights are adaptively assigned according to the cosine similarity between the gradients of multiple surrogate models. This guides the patch optimization toward the commonly vulnerable regions shared by multiple models, reduces gradient conflicts, and enhances the transferability of the patch. Experimental results on multiple datasets demonstrate that the proposed method significantly reduces the detection accuracy of ship targets. When integrating multiple detectors, the generated patch achieves approximately 60% mAP reduction and 50% target vanishing rate on three unknown black-box detection models. Furthermore, in multiclass attack scenarios, the proposed method also achieves an attack success rate of around 40%, showing strong transferability.
author: Qi Wang, Jiahao Cui, Keyi Zhang, Lingquan Meng, Haifeng Li, Chengli Peng

title: AssociativeFormer: Integrating Sparse Memory Attention and Versatile Tokenization for Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: Developing hyperspectral image classification models has long been hindered by problems regarding spectral redundancy and complexity, as well as the limitations of previously proposed transformer methods. We present AssociativeFormer, an architecture designed to address hyperspectral analysis by merging memory association and efficient spatial-spectral processing. New features of the architecture include: Sparse memory attention with Top-K Routing that reduces computational complexity from quadratic to linear scaling while retaining its ability to discriminate effectively. The versatile convolution tokenization allows the architecture to capture multiscale spatial-spectral features and filters from dynamically generated convolution tokens. The associative memory provided by a Hopfield network allows real time storage and retrieval of patterns, while the Gompertz lar unit activation decreases impediments on the gradient flow to create a more useful representation of the features. The network's global workspace design preserves interlayer information for independent flow and spatial-spectral dependency tracking. The AssociativeFormer model delivers state-of-the-art accuracy with linear complexity and competitive inference speed, providing a superior accuracy–efficiency tradeoff compared to existing hyperspectral classification methods.
author: Ayesha Shafique, Mohammad Siraj, Saad Sohail, Saif A. Alsaif, Benmao Cheng

title: SHARP: A Scalable FPGA-Based CNN Accelerator for On-Board Target Detection
content: On-board target detection demands high computational throughput under stringent energy and memory budgets, thereby necessitating architectures that tightly couple high parallelism to memory-efficient data orchestration. This article presents SHARP, a scalable field-programmable gate array (FPGA)-oriented convolutional neural network (CNN) accelerator tailored for real-time, resource-constrained remote-sensing deployments. SHARP increases sustained concurrency by adopting a packet-based multilevel parallel convolution strategy that reduces intermediate storage while enabling high-degree parallel processing of large multichannel feature maps. Building on this compute fabric, a scalable multilevel parallel CNN compute engine is provided so that the effective parallelism can be tuned to match available digital signal processor (DSP), lookup table, and block random access memory (BRAM) resources; this flexibility enables efficient mapping across devices with different resource budgets. To preserve throughput in practice, an integrated off-chip and on-chip data access strategy combines DDR interleaving with multi-BRAM dislocated caching and ping-pong buffering, thereby improving burst bandwidth utilization and removing read/write conflicts so I/O can be overlapped with computation. A theoretical resource-analysis framework ties these elements together by quantifying achievable parallelism under DSP and BRAM constraints and guiding hardware dimensioning. SHARP is implemented on a Xilinx XC7VX690T. The prototype attains a peak throughput of 1480.95 GOPS at 250 MHz with 20.525 W power and delivers 72.15 GOPS/W energy efficiency while maintaining 98.47% mAP. Compared with reported FPGA YOLO accelerators, these results demonstrate that SHARP achieves a superior tradeoff between throughput and energy efficiency for real-time, on-board remote-sensing applications.
author: Ming Xu, Liang Chen, Hao Shi, Yuxin Liu, Wenchao Liu

title: Extending Accurate Geolocation From Land to Ocean Using Automated Cross-Sensor GCP Extraction and Stripwise Offset Modeling for VHR Optical Imagery
content: Accurate geolocation of maritime vessels in very high resolution (VHR) optical satellite imagery is challenging in regions that lack reliable ground control points (GCPs). Conventional geometric correction approaches rely on manually selected GCPs or coastal features, such as shorelines and infrastructure, which are unevenly distributed and often unavailable across most open-ocean environments. This study presents an end-to-end framework that automates cross-sensor GCP extraction and models stripwise geometric offsets to enable reliable geolocation refinement in GCP-limited maritime scenes. The method integrates OmniGlue-based cross-domain matching, density-consistent inlier filtering using hierarchical density-based spatial clustering of applications with noise, and physically interpretable, attitude-linked offset modeling to characterize deterministic along-track geometric trends across long satellite strips. Experiments on 70 scenes from four VHR strips show that automated GCPs achieve accuracy comparable with manual selection, reducing root-mean-square error (RMSE) from 45 pixels to 5 pixels. When applied to expanded automated-GCP sets, affine correction reaches four-pixel RMSE, while physical calibration achieves three-pixel RMSE. Stripwise offset models further exhibit strong extrapolation capability, predicting x-offsets for up to 11 scenes from a single land scene with a 2-pixel RMSE, and y-offsets for up to 9 scenes from four scenes with a 5-pixel RMSE. Automatic identification system overlays provide qualitative confirmation of improved alignment in offshore regions lacking local control. The proposed framework provides a scalable and fully automated solution for geometric correction of long-strip optical imagery, enabling reliable geolocation refinement in, otherwise, GCP-free maritime scenes.
author: Keehyun Um, Hyoseon Jang, Hong-Gyoo Sohn

title: Extraction of Habitat Suitability for Fall Armyworm in East Africa Considering Phenological Information
content: The fall armyworm (Spodoptera frugiperda), a highly polyphagous and destructive agricultural pest, has posed severe threats to food security in East Africa since its invasion. Identifying habitat suitability for the fall armyworm is critical for early prevention and control. However, existing studies have not adequately incorporated phenological information, which limits the accuracy of habitat suitability extraction. This study constructed a dataset integrating phenological and other environmental factors. Using both historical occurrence records and pseudoabsence points generated by the “E-H” method, habitat suitability models were developed based on four machine learning algorithms (SVM, RF, XGBoost, and LightGBM) to extract the habitat suitability for the fall armyworm in East Africa from January to December 2022. The results showed that LightGBM outperformed other models across multiple evaluation metrics, achieving an accuracy of 85.7%, an Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve of 0.936, a Kappa value of 0.871, and a true skill statistic of 0.821. Compared to models without phenological information, the improved model significantly enhanced the accuracy of habitat suitability extraction and better captured the potential distribution of the fall armyworm. By incorporating phenological data, the study also conducted a detailed analysis of the generational dynamics of the fall armyworm in East Africa, providing valuable insights for control measures. Additionally, habitat suitability extraction at a finer scale was performed using Sustainable Development Goals Science Satellite 1 thermal infrared spectrometer data, which preliminarily validated its stability and superiority in fall armyworm habitat suitability applications. These findings offer scientific support for food security and sustainable agricultural development.
author: Minghao Wang, Wenjiang Huang, Yingying Dong, Yanru Huang, Linyi Liu, Xiaoping Du, Changping Huang, Kun Wang, Yan Zhang, Xuanli He

title: A UAV-Based Image Dataset and a Novel Morphological Feature Extraction Framework for Identifying Ground Fissures Caused by Coal Mining Activities
content: Ground fissures induced by coal mining activities pose severe risks such as coal fires, water inrush, and environmental degradation. Efficient identification of these fissures is critical for disaster prevention and mining safety. However, the lack of publicly training data tailored to mining scenarios hinders progress in deep-learning-based fissure identification. This article presents the ground fissures in mining areas (GFM) dataset, the first publicly available high-resolution uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV) image dataset for ground fissure segmentation in coal mining areas, comprising 7368 annotated images across diverse landscapes and introducing a structured fissure categorization scheme. Furthermore, we propose FS-YOLO, a specialized instance segmentation framework that incorporates the dynamic snake convolutional pyramid pooling and deformable large kernel attention modules to enhance learning multiscale morphological features for ground fissure segmentation. Experiments indicate that FS-YOLO outperforms YOLOv8 by 3.5% and 6.5% in box AP@0.5 and box AP@0.95, and by 4.8% and 3.6% in mask AP@0.5 and mask AP@0.95, respectively, and achieves competitive results against other state-of-the-art methods.
author: Zhihua Xu, Yunhao Lin, Chao Sun, Hao Jiang, Zhenxin Zhang, Keming Yang, Ximin Cui, Jincheng Jiang, Yuling Zhao, Suping Peng

title: A Geometric Structure-Aware Morphology–Pose Decoupled Framework for Ship Detection in Remote-Sensing Imagery
content: Accurate ship detection in remote sensing imagery is a key technological foundation for maritime surveillance, traffic management, and national defense. However, coupled geometric variations, loss of fine structural details, and isotropic regression bias under horizontal bounding box (HBB) supervision still hinder detection accuracy, especially under large-scale variations and complex backgrounds. To address these challenges, this article proposes a geometric structure-aware detection transformer (GSA-DETR), which improves detection accuracy while maintaining favorable model complexity by explicitly disentangling geometric factors and enforcing structural constraints during learning. Specifically, a morphology–pose decoupled fusion module is proposed to construct decoupled yet complementary representations, suppressing morphology–pose coupling interference and enhancing structural consistency across scales. Meanwhile, a selective frequency reconstruction module is devised to model and reconstruct low- and high-frequency components separately, alleviating edge smoothing induced by conventional upsampling and improving texture-detail recovery for small-scale ships. In addition, an anisotropic geometric regularization strategy is designed to mitigate isotropic bias in HBB regression by introducing shape-dependent anisotropic constraints, thereby enhancing geometry-consistent localization robustness for elongated ships without predicting or recovering instance-level orientation. Extensive experiments on the DIOR and DOTA public datasets demonstrate that GSA-DETR consistently outperforms representative convolutional neural network- and Transformer-based detectors in detection accuracy and robustness.
author: Huan Liu, Yibo Liu, Xin Cheng, Feng Wen, Xu Wang, Zhiliang Hu, Ning Xu

title: CGM2Net: Cloud-Guided Mamba-Based Multitask Network for Multimodal Remote Sensing Semantic Segmentation and Cloud Removal
content: Semantic segmentation and cloud removal via optical-Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) fusion are crucial tasks in Earth observation. Existing works have proposed multitask architectures to simultaneously address segmentation and cloud removal, but they lack targeted modeling of under-cloud features, leading to blurred ground-object reconstruction and poor fine-grained recognition performance in thick-cloud regions. To address these challenges, this article proposes a Cloud-Guided Mamba-based Multitask Network (CGM2Net) for end-to-end multimodal segmentation and cloud removal. Specifically, CGM2Net utilizes a dual-stream encoder and integrates three key modules at each stage: cloud-guided feature restoration module, which, under cloud-mask-guided gating, exploits spatial- and channel-wise cross-modal correlations to reconstruct sub-cloud optical features and suppress SAR speckle; cloud-guided semantic interaction module, which performs mask-aware state-space modulation to enable selective, region-aware cross-modal semantic exchange; and Mamba-Based Fusion module, which adaptively fuses enhanced multimodal features to fully exploit modal complementary information. Two task-specific decoders synergistically optimize segmentation and cloud removal, thereby promoting mutual enhancement between the semantic prior and visual restoration. Experiments on M3M-CR and LuojiaSET-OSFCR demonstrate that CGM2Net achieves state-of-the-art performance on both tasks. Ablation studies and feature visualizations further validate the complementary roles and effectiveness of the proposed modules.
author: Jianhao Li, Jue Wang, Hao Shi, Shan Dong, Wenchao Liu, Liang Chen, Wei Li

title: ALS Teacher: Active Label Selection for Semisupervised Oriented Object Detection in Remote Sensing Imagery
content: Semisupervised object detection (SSOD) in remote sensing scenarios is fundamentally challenged by orientation-sensitive pseudolabel noise under limited supervision, where arbitrary object rotations and complex spatial distributions significantly degrade label reliability. Although SSOD has shown promising results in natural images, existing methods predominantly focus on horizontal object detection and struggle to generalize to oriented settings. In this article, we present ALS Teacher, a semisupervised framework specifically designed for oriented object detection within a two-stage detector. To improve the reliability of pseudolabels under limited supervision, we introduce an active label selection strategy (ALSS), which identifies high-value labeled samples using comprehensive selection metrics. Furthermore, to enhance orientation-aware representation learning from unlabeled data, we develop orientation-preserving interpolation augmentation, a geometry-consistent interpolation augmentation strategy designed to preserve spatial coherence and rotational characteristics. In addition, we propose the global oriented loss function, which incorporates global spatial distributions into oriented regression, fostering more stable and expressive orientation modeling in the student detector. Extensive experiments on DOTA-v1.5 and CODrone benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework. With only 10% labeled data, our method achieves 52.26% mAP on DOTA-v1.5 and 37.84% mAP on CODrone, surpassing the supervised Oriented R-CNN baseline by 7.78% and 4.37% mAP, respectively. These results highlight the potential of our approach as a scalable solution for semisupervised oriented object detection in complex remote sensing imagery.
author: Yongfei Xian, Haopeng Zhang, Kangning Wang, Yanlei Wen, Zhiguo Jiang

title: Machine Learning and Satellite Time-Series Reveal Climate-Driven Variability in Dune Migration in the Western Sahara
content: This study demonstrates the potential of advanced machine learning techniques, combined with Google Earth Engine and JavaScript, for detecting and analyzing barchan dunes from satellite imagery. The proposed approach enables accurate dune identification and subsequent estimation of migration rates and directions. Climatic factor analysis revealed conditions that accelerate dune migration and identified which dunes are more sensitive to environmental changes. Temporal patterns of dune migration show distinct peaks in movement speed, strongly associated with increases in average wind velocity. The propagation direction of barchan dunes correlates closely with the prevailing wind direction. Spatially, the highest migration rates occur in the northern part of the erg, where unobstructed wind flow promotes higher speeds. Furthermore, isolated dunes migrate faster than clustered dunes, where inter-dune distances are below 300 m. Dunes within clusters exhibit greater stability, likely due to mutual aerodynamic interactions that limit movement. Wind speed emerged as the dominant driver of dune migration, while soil and air moisture exert a stabilizing effect—migration slows under humid conditions and accelerates during dry periods. Elevated sediment temperatures also favor migration, likely due to surface desiccation. Overall, dune migration is a cyclic process influenced by climatic variability, with increased movement during dry, windy years. These findings highlight the importance of integrating remote sensing, machine learning, and climatic data to improve understanding of dune dynamics and their response to environmental forcing.
author: Patryk Sapiega

title: DEAE-INR: Dual Encoder Based Autoencoders With Implicit Neural Representations for Seismic Random Noise Attenuation
content: Seismic random noise attenuation is an essential step in seismic data processing. Noise in seismic data may degrade their quality, which leads to misinterpretation of Earth's subsurface structures. Therefore, random noise in seismic data needs to be eliminated effectively for accurate subsurface structure interpretation. Recently, autoencoder (AE)-based random noise attenuation has been extensively studied. Earlier, an AE with a correlation coefficient loss function was used to attenuate random noise in seismic data. The correlation coefficient is a second-order function that does not completely eliminate random noise in seismic data. AE may struggle to capture complex spatial and temporal patterns in seismic data. Therefore, to address these limitations, we propose a dual encoder-based autoencoders with implicit neural representations (DEAE-INR), which use the $\ell _{2,1}$ and orthogonal loss functions to attenuate random noise while efficiently retaining seismic signal features. Here, a dual encoder is used for diverse feature extraction, an INR for continuous signal modeling, and an adaptive fusion mechanism for optimal reconstruction of denoised seismic data. Subsequently, in proposed DEAE-INR network is trained using clean and noisy synthetic data, then field seismic data are denoised in an unsupervised inference manner using a pretrained model. To assess the performance of the proposed method, we used metrics such as mean squared error (MSE), signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and energy. The proposed method resulted in higher SNR, lower MSE, and minimal energy values on synthetic and field datasets.
author: Nakka Shekhar, Karthikeyan Elumalai

title: GRO-YOLO: Multigranularity Perception for Efficient Small Object Detection From Remote Sensing Images
content: Detecting small objects in remote sensing images remains a challenging task due to the submergence of weak features in complex backgrounds, which is even harder under limited computational and storage resources. Existing detection algorithms often struggle to achieve an optimal tradeoff between lightweight deployment and high detection precision. To address this issue, this article proposes GRO-YOLO, a lightweight detector based on a global-region-object multigranularity perception architecture. First, asymmetric fusion neck is designed to maximize the retention of spatial texture information for small objects, simultaneously, reduce computational redundancy. Second, to achieve high-precision detection under lightweight constraints, we construct a Magnitude-Aware Global Context module and a dense context-aware block. The former captures long-range context dependencies with linear computational complexity by introducing a magnitude-aware mechanism, while the latter reinforces the semantic density of local features via gating mechanisms. Furthermore, addressing the difficulty of separating small objects, a sparse parameter Gaussian-guided tiny object enhancement (SPGT) mechanism is proposed. Based on information theory, SPGT constructs target-specific saliency ground-truth maps for auxiliary supervision during the training phase, significantly improving the model’s sensitivity to small objects without increasing inference costs. Extensive experiments on three public datasets, including: VisDrone2019, USOD, and RSOD, demonstrate that GRO-YOLO, with only approximately 6 M parameters, outperforms state-of-the-art methods, such as YOLO11 and FFCA-YOLO, in both detection accuracy and inference speed, achieving balance between effectiveness and efficiency.
author: Yongyuan Liao, Yiliang Li, Zekai He, Chenyu Zhang, Yu Wan, Yujie Wang, Yansheng Li

title: An Explainable Deep Forest Approach for Modeling Actual Evapotranspiration in Arid Northwest China Using Multisource Data
content: Accurate estimation of actual evapotranspiration (ETa) in arid regions is essential for effective water resource management. Despite notable advances in ETa modeling, limited research has systematically addressed the joint optimization of variable selection, model development, and interpretability. To fill this gap, we performed variables selection through correlation analysis and design eight input strategies that integrate key meteorological and remote sensing variables. Five state-of-the-art machine learning models–Artificial Neural Network (ANN), Random Forest (RF), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), Tabular Neural Network (TabNet), and Deep Forest (DF)–are employed for ETa prediction. To enhance model interpretability, we apply SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) and Permutation Importance to quantify the contribution of each input variable. Our findings demonstrate that the DF model combined with the S6 input strategy (a combination of six key meteorological and remote sensing variables), denoted as DF6, delivers the best predictive performance. Leveraging this optimal configuration, we generate a high-resolution ETa dataset for Northwest China, spanning from 2001 to 2024 at 8-day intervals with 5 km spatial resolution. Interpretability analysis highlights net radiation (Rn) as the most influential factor, exhibiting a strong positive effect on ETa. Furthermore, Mann-Kendall trend analysis reveals a significant upward trend in ETa, particularly in the northwestern and southeastern subregions. This study presents a robust and interpretable modeling framework that improves ETa estimation under data-limited conditions and supports long-term hydrological analysis and sustainable water management in arid regions.
author: Yuan Wu, Haibo Wang, Yi'na Hu, Xufeng Wang, Kaixuan Liu, Liying Geng, Junlei Tan

title: An Adaptive Homogeneous Pixel-Offset Tracking Technique Integrated With SAR Change Detection: A Case Study of Glacier Displacement Monitoring at Namcha Barwa in the Himalayas
content: Global warming has accelerated glacier and snowmelt, leading to hazards such as debris flows and glacial lake outburst floods. Accurate time-series monitoring of glacier deformation is essential for disaster early warning and glacier dynamics research. However, single-band synthetic aperture radar (SAR) intensity imagery is affected by speckle noise and limited feature dimensionality, which restricts change detection in high-noise environments. Conventional pixel offset tracking (POT) methods suffer from decorrelation caused by heterogeneous motion within matching windows, resulting in mismatches and loss of glacier boundary details. To address these limitations, two methodological improvements are proposed in this study. First, a differential spatio-temporal attention (DSTA) module is designed for multitemporal SAR intensity imagery. It integrates dual-branch multiscale convolutions, differential weighting, and residual fusion to enhance change representation between image pairs. The DSTA module is embedded into a TransUNet architecture to construct the DSTA-TransUNet model for glacier change detection. Second, a change-detection-mask-based adaptive homogeneous pixel offset tracking (CDM-APOT) method is developed. It improves offset estimation by constructing homogeneous pixel matching windows and applying a signal-to-noise-ratio-adaptive window expansion strategy. Experiments on the Namcha Barwa Glacier show that DSTA-TransUNet improves pixel accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and IoU by 1.13%, 0.61%, 2.55%, 1.59%, and 2.91%, respectively, compared with the baseline TransUNet. Compared with conventional POT methods, CDM-APOT increases monitoring accuracy by more than 50% and significantly improves glacier displacement field completeness. The proposed framework integrates deep learning-based change detection with adaptive offset tracking, providing a robust solution for high-precision glacier motion monitoring in complex mountainous regions.
author: Zewei Liu, Chengsheng Yang, Tianli Sun, Ziqian Wang, Xinshuo Li, Pezi He, Yi Yu, Hao He, Huilan Ding

title: Deep Semantic-Attention Proxy Hashing With Coarse-to-Fine Representation for Multilabel Remote Sensing Image Retrieval
content: Deep hashing techniques are widely used in remote sensing image retrieval due to their fast retrieval and low cost. However, existing multilabel retrieval methods encounter two main challenges. First, the input sample pixels commonly include irrelevant backgrounds, degrading semantic feature representation. Second, traditional vision transformer models directly adopt fine-grained feature extraction, which generates redundant long sequences and significantly increases computational costs. To address the mentioned problems, a deep semantic-attention proxy hashing framework (DSaPH) is proposed for multilabel remote sensing image retrieval. Specifically, to achieve the dynamic feature extraction of images, a coarse-to-fine representation module with two stages of coarse segmentation and fine segmentation is proposed, which identifies the object-related patches in the coarse-grained segmentation through critical region detection and aggregates the global features of the original image in combination with the feature reuse mechanism. To exclude background interference and focus on object information, the object-focus loss highlights the object information through Grad-CAM and generates object-fused image-focus hash codes. Furthermore, class-related proxy learning is introduced to use proxy hash codes to supervise the semantic representation of object-fused hash codes, and embed proxy hash codes and object-fused hash codes into a unified Hamming space to capture the Hamming similarity of neighboring multilabel data. Extensive experimental evaluations on three benchmark datasets show that our proposed DSaPH framework achieves excellent performance in multilabel remote sensing image retrieval. The code for our DSaPH framework is publicly available at https://github.com/QinLab-WFU/DSaPH.git.
author: Chunyu Yan, Lei Wang, Qibing Qin, Lei Huang, Wenfeng Zhang

title: Real-Time Estimation of Tropospheric Delay Measurements Using a Low-Cost Weather Station Integrated With the Hopfield Model
content: This study presents preliminary results of the development of a meteorological microstation that calculates and can correct the tropospheric delay in the precise point positioning method with a global navigation satellite system (GNSS). Using the Hopfield model fed with atmospheric data collected by a BME280 sensor, zenith tropospheric delay (ZTD) was estimated based on temperature and pressure, and this ZTD was compared with the ZTD measured by GNSS signals processed on the UTC(LRTE) time scale. The tests were conducted over a period of 38 days using both the GPS and GLONASS satellite constellations. Time-series analysis confirms that models based on locally collected data reduced the noise amplitude associated with the GPS constellation by 98.74% and 99.67% for the GLONASS constellation, significantly improving the accuracy of applications that require high-precision positioning, as well as time and frequency synchronization.
author: Vitor S. Tavares, Luiz P. S. Damaceno, Luiz V. G. Tarelho, Daniel V. Magalhães

title: HyTC-TaNet: A Hybrid Deep Learning Model Capturing Multiday Temporal Dependencies for Daily Mean Air Temperature Estimation With Spatial Applicability Analysis
content: Air temperature is a fundamental indicator for climate monitoring, agricultural planning, and ecosystem management. Land surface temperature (LST) retrieved from thermal remote sensing is widely used as a critical proxy for air temperature. However, existing air temperature estimation studies predominantly rely on single-date LST and diurnal variations (e.g., the diurnal temperature cycle, DTC), overlooking the potential of multiday LST temporal dependencies and lacking explicit spatial representativeness assessments. To address these challenges, here we present a hybrid transformer-CNN daily mean air temperature (Ta) estimation network (HyTC-TaNet), which integrates Transformer-based temporal attention with convolutional feature extraction. While the area of applicability (AOA) metric is introduced to quantify spatial prediction confidence. Comparative experiments involving eight models, including HyTC-TaNet and seven benchmark algorithms, reveal that a 6–7 days temporal input window yields the best performance across all models. HyTC-TaNet achieves the highest accuracy, with RMSE = 1.429 $^{\circ }$C, MAE = 1.101 $^{\circ }$C, and R$^{2}$ = 0.976, reducing errors by 0.273 $^{\circ }$C (RMSE) and 0.219 $^{\circ }$C (MAE) compared with concurrent day input. Spatial-temporal analysis further confirms that the optimal temporal sequence enriches historical and trend information, significantly enhancing estimation stability and spatial detail. Furthermore, the AOA of the model was assessed, confirming robust applicability in station-dense plains, while effectively flagging extrapolation risks over large water bodies and high-altitude ridges due to limited representativeness in the training data. These findings demonstrate the strong potential of coupling HyTC-TaNet with optimally selected multiday time series and spatial applicability assessment for precise, scalable air temperature estimation. The code will be made available on https://github.com/wanfengna/HyTC-TaNet.
author: Li Liu, Cian Yuan, Jingfeng Huang, Yi Yu, Pan Shao, Junbo Yu, Lu Wang, Ran Huang, Dong Ren, Thomas F. A. Bishop

title: Cross-Sectional Complex Permittivity Imaging by Hybrid Scheme of Radar and Tomography for Microwave Nondestructive Pavement Inspection
content: This study proposes a cross-sectional 3-D complex permittivity imaging method for microwave pavement inspection, integrating radar imaging with contrast source inversion (CSI)-based tomography to mitigate the ill-posed nature of fully 3-D inverse scattering. Cross-sectional quantitative imaging exploits the radar images to limit unknowns in the 3-D inversion, while the CSI-based region-of-interest optimization improves the reconstruction of both the geometries and complex permittivities of anomalies such as air- or water-filled cavities buried in pavement area. Numerical tests, using a fully 3-D forward solver based on the finite-difference time-domain method, demonstrated that the proposed hybrid scheme can successfully reconstruct a quantitative profile of the complex permittivity of buried objects, even under limited observation conditions.
author: Hiroshi Inoue, Shouhei Kidera

title: DQA-DETR: Dynamic Query Aggregation for Oriented Object Detection in Remote Sensing Images
content: Oriented object detection in remote sensing images faces challenges, such as arbitrary object orientations and uneven distribution of object quantities. Although detection transformer (DETR)-based methods have recently achieved promising progress in remote sensing object detection, they still face challenges when using a fixed query budget in scenes where object quantities vary significantly due to large-scale imaging and complex spatial distributions. A fixed query budget can be poorly matched to such variability, producing redundant queries in sparse scenes and many high-quality negatives under one-to-one matching, which may hinder optimization efficiency and degrade performance. To address this issue, we propose a transformer-based dynamic query aggregation detection framework (DQA-DETR) to alleviate query redundancy in remote sensing scenarios. DQA-DETR incorporates three key modules: aggregation center predictor, aggregation center selector, and query aggregator, which are responsible for predicting the required number of representative queries, selecting high-quality aggregation centers, and aggregating semantically related queries via a multihead attention mechanism, respectively. This strategy adaptively adjusts the number of queries involved in matching according to the object quantity in each image, effectively reducing redundancy and enhancing the model’s adaptability in both sparse and dense scenes. Extensive experiments on the DOTA v1.0 and v1.5 datasets demonstrate that DQA-DETR improves detection performance, while maintaining the end-to-end advantages of DETR, exhibiting strong competitiveness among state-of-the-art methods.
author: Yongchen Yao, Songwei Pei, Yuanzhou Huang, Qian Li, Shangguang Wang

title: Adaptive Fusion of Aspect Constraint and Strain Model for InSAR 3-D Deformation Inversion
content: Accurate and high-resolution monitoring of 3-D surface deformation is essential for analyzing and mitigating geological hazards. Interferometric synthetic aperture radar (InSAR) has become a pivotal technique for monitoring surface deformation due to its unique advantages. However, the side-looking imaging geometry of near-polar orbiting satellites results in InSAR line-of-sight (LOS) measurements having severe insensitivity to the North–South (N-S) deformation component. Consequently, InSAR-LOS 3-D deformation inversion becomes an inherently ill-posed problem, severely restricting its application in the precise identification of geological hazards in mountainous areas. To address this challenge, this article proposes a novel InSAR-LOS 3-D deformation inversion method fusing terrain aspect constraints and strain models. This method integrates the aspect constraint and the strain model into a unified inversion framework, simultaneously leveraging the physical pointing of topography information and the spatial smoothness of the strain model to provide dual prior support for 3-D deformation inversion. Furthermore, specifically targeting terrain-sensitive regions with aspects near the N-S direction, the method introduces an adaptive window selection and dynamic regularization adjustment strategy, designed to mitigate local ill-posedness and enhance estimation accuracy. Simulation experiments confirm that the proposed method yields both a more accurate reconstruction and a higher fidelity surface deformation pattern than traditional approaches. Practical application in the Panzhou area, Guizhou, further verifies the robustness of the method: the proposed method successfully recovered a reasonable 3-D deformation field consistent with the topographic mechanism, with solution uncertainty significantly lower than that of comparative methods. This study confirms that the intelligent fusion of multisource physical priors is an effective approach to overcome the inherent geometric defects of InSAR-LOS observations, thereby directly achieving high-precision and physically credible 3-D deformation retrieval, making the method particularly effective for monitoring gravity-driven deformation in complex terrain.
author: Yiming Chen, Yunjia Wang, Feng Zhao, Sen Du, Kewei Zhang, Kesheng Huang

title: Radiometric Observation of Brightness Temperature Fluctuations on a Clear Summer Day in the Mid-latitudes
content: Ground-based microwave radiometers (MWRs), as fundamental remote sensing instruments, measure the brightness temperature (BT), which is used to retrieve key atmospheric parameters. Accurate retrievals require understanding the uncertainties in BT and identifying potential anomalies. This study aims to characterize BT fluctuations during a clear summer day in the mid-latitudes with three MWRs and to explore their causes using remote sensing and in situ techniques, including Raman lidar, Doppler lidar, radar wind profiler, radiosonde, and weather station. BT fluctuations are detected from the time series and analyzed using highpass filtering, fast Fourier transform, and wavelet transforms to quantify their amplitude and frequency. Multiple instrument observations are then used to describe the associated atmospheric conditions. It was found that BT at the 21- and 31-GHz channels shows significant increased fluctuations between 12:00 and 16:00 UT. The dominant spectral feature indicates a periodicity of approximately 7 min during the BT fluctuations. Multiinstrument observations suggest that abundant moisture, strong heating, and alternating updrafts and downdrafts in the planetary boundary layer create conditions favorable for water vapor convection and turbulence, which are likely responsible for the BT fluctuations. Understanding such fluctuations is essential for improving atmospheric retrievals from MWRs and enhancing their assimilation in numerical weather prediction models. Further, the measurement of BT fluctuations by microwave radiometry could be useful for detection of turbulence.
author: Wenyue Wang, Axel Murk, Maxime Hervo, Giovanni Martucci, Klemens Hocke

title: Chip Industry Week In Review
content:
Manufacturing

  • SpaceX filed documents for a “Terafab” semiconductor manufacturing and computing facility at Gibbons Creek Reservoir in Texas, with an initial investment of~$55B, and a possible total investment of up to $119B.
  • China is aiming to use 70% domestic chips by the end of this year, reports Nikkei Asia.
  • Apple reportedly held early-stage talks with Intel and Samsung about producing main device processors in the U.S., as the company explores additional domestic manufacturing options beyond TSMC, according to Reuters. Discussions point to continued pressure on major chip customers to diversify advanced-node supply chains amid capacity, geopolitical, and U.S. manufacturing concerns.

Big Deals

  • Applied Materials plans to buy ASMPT‘s NEXX business, a supplier of large-area advanced packaging deposition equipment.
  • Lattice will acquire firmware developer AMI for ~$1.7B to create a secure management and control platform.
  • Siemens partnered with Xometry to develop an AI-native marketplace to bring together suppliers and buyers of custom manufacturing. Siemens also invested about $50M into Xometry, and noted it has invested $1B in U.S. manufacturing over the last 5 years.
  • Nvidia and IREN inked a deal to deploy up to 5 GW of Nvidia’s DSX-aligned AI infrastructure across IREN’s Sweetwater, Texas campus, with NVIDIA receiving rights to invest up to $2.1B.

Innovations

  • Nvidia, PulteGroup, and Span are putting mini data centers in homes to take advantage of unused local grid electrical capacity.
  • Panthalassa raised $140M to complete a pilot fab in Oregon and accelerate deployment of AI inferencing at sea using power generated from ocean waves.
  • GF introduced its optical module solution for co-packaged optics (CPO), built with its own silicon photonics technology.
  • ST expects to generate more than $3B in cumulative space-related semiconductor revenue from 2026 through 2028, driven by demand for chips used in low-Earth orbit satellite networks.

Europe

  • CHIPDIPLO launched the EU Chips Ecosystem Tracker, an interactive map of more than 2,500 semiconductor companies across the EU.
  • CEOs from ASML, Siemens, and five other tech companies banded together in an opinion piece arguing Europe must simplify its digital rules, scale homegrown technologies such as semiconductors and AI, and act faster to protect its global competitiveness and technological sovereignty.

Reports

  • Earnings: Arm, Infineon, GlobalFoundries, Onto Innovation, PDF Solutions, Solstice Advanced Materials, Synaptics, and Silvaco.
  • Global semiconductor sales reached ~$299B in Q1 2026, up 25% from Q4 2025, reports SIA. March worldwide sales were ~$100B, increasing 79% year-over-year and ~12% sequentially, with growth across all major regions.

Research

  • An MIT team created a silicon-photonics lidar chip design that significantly lowers signal crosstalk, enabling wider field of view, compact solid-state lidar sensors for AVs, drones, and other sensing systems.
  • UC Berkeley-led researchers transformed titanium dioxide (TiO₂) into a ferroelectric material by reducing its thickness to less than 3nm, opening a path to ultra-scaled, energy-efficient electronic devices.

Quick links to more news:

Global
In-Depth
Reports and Deals
